question: The sculpture with the three faces of Brahma, Vishnu and Mahesh know as Trimurti appears in.......... (UPSC Prelims 1979)
option_1: Ajanta caves
option_2: Ellora caves
option_3: Kalva caves
option_4: Elephanta caves
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • The sculpture of Trimurti, which depicts the three faces of Brahma, Vishnu, and Mahesh, is located in the Elephanta Caves.
  • The Elephanta Caves are a group of seven caves that are a UNESCO World Heritage Site.The caves are primarily dedicated to the Hindu god Shiva and contain rock cut stone sculptures that blend Hindu and Buddhist ideas and iconography.
  • The Trimurti is a large stone sculpture located in the first cave of the Elephanta Caves.It is considered the most important sculpture in the caves and is described as a “masterpiece of Gupta-Chalukyan art”.The sculpture is located at the back of the cave, facing the entrance, and is also known as Maheshmurti or Trimurti Sadashiva.
  • The sculpture represents three aspects of Shiva : the Creator, the Preserver, and the Destroyer.The left half of the sculpture represents Aghora or Bhairava, the central full face represents Taptapurusha or Mahadeva, and the right half represents Vamadeva or Uma.

question: The workship of idols started in India in.......... (UPSC Prelims 1979)
option_1: Pre-Aryan period
option_2: Gupta period
option_3: Maurya period
option_4: Kushan period
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • In Hinduism, idol worship became a common feature during the Gupta period. The early Aryans only practiced nature worship and elaborate rituals.
  • Some say that idol worship is a central part of the ancient period and plays an important role in Hinduism. Others say that idol worship is just one way for prayer and meditation.
  • The pre-Aryan period : in India is the time before the arrival of the Aryans, which is estimated to have been around 4,000 years ago. The pre-Aryan period includes the Indus Valley Civilization, which was the first urban civilization on the subcontinent.

The pre-Aryan period is divided into several stages based on human development:

1. Paleolithic Period: (300,000 - 8,000 BC), Old Stone Age

2. Mesolithic Period: (8,000–4,000 BC), Late Stone Age

3. Neolithic Period: (4,000–1,800 BC), New Stone Age

4. Copper and Bronze Age: (1,800 1,000 BC)

The pre-Aryan period includes at least three different ethnic types:

1. Austro-Asians: The earliest people related to Australian aborigines.

2. Dravidians: The people that the Aryans encountered.

3. Aryans: The Caucasian steppe people who came to India around 4,000 years ago.

The Maurya Empire :

  • It was a powerful empire that ruled over the Indian subcontinent from 321–185 BC. The empire was founded by Chandragupta Maurya in 322 BC and lasted until 185 BC. The empire included parts of present day India, Pakistan, Bangladesh, Nepal, Bhutan, and Afghanistan, as well as parts of Iran and Myanmar.
  • The Maurya Empire was a centralized monarchy with a vast bureaucracy. Chandragupta Maurya established a single currency, a network of regional governors and administrators, and a civil service to provide security and justice for merchants, farmers, and traders.
  • The Maurya Empire was a time of economic prosperity and cultural development. Mauryan art and architecture promoted the spread of Buddhism, and many structures were built for religious purposes. The sculptures of the Mauryan Age are considered some of the finest art in India.

The Kushan Empire :

  • It was a powerful political force in the Eurasian-African world from the 1st century BCE to the 3rd century CE. The Kushan Empire was a significant power alongside the Roman Empire, the Parthian Empire, and the Han Empire.
  • The Kushans were nomadic people who lived in the Central Asian steppes. In the early 1st century CE, they established a powerful empire that stretched from modern-day Afghanistan to northern India.

The Kushan Empire was administered from two capitals:

1) Purushapura (modern-day Peshawar in Pakistan) and

2) Mathura in Northern India.

  • The Kushans were originally foreign invaders, but they became completely Indianized in their ways and culture. The Kushan Empire controlled large parts of the Silk Route, which led to the spread of Buddhism into China, Korea, and Japan. The Kushan Empire is less recognized in current world historiography.

question: Which of the following throws light on Harappan Culture? (UPSC Prelims 1979)
option_1: Arechacological excavations
option_2: The script on copper sheets
option_3: Rock edicts
option_4: All of the above
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Archaeological excavations provide important information about the Harappan culture.
  • Archaeological excavations in the 19th and 20th centuries have provided data about the Harappan culture’s technology, art, trade, transportation, writing, and religion.
  • The first thing found in Harappa was a stone seal with a unicorn picture and some words on it. More seals with different animals and writing have been found in the area.
  • The Seals of Pashupati Mahadeva may shed light on the religion of the Harappan age. The seal shows a seated figure of a Yogi, probably Shiva Pashupati, surrounded by four animals.

What is Harappan Civilization?

  • The Harappan Civilization, also known as the Indus Valley Civilization, was a Bronze Age civilization that lasted from 3300 1300 BCE. It was the earliest known urban culture in the Indian subcontinent and was located in modern-day India and Pakistan.

The Harappan Civilization made many technological advances, including:

  1. Standardized weights and measures
  2. Seal carving
  3. Metallurgy with copper, bronze, lead, and tin
  4. Fire-baked bricks
  • The Harappan Civilization is also known for its highly developed urban planning. Houses were one or two-storeyed and had separate bathing areas, wells, and proper drainage systems.
  • The Harappan Civilization is considered a Bronze Age civilization because many objects have been found that are made up of copper based alloys.
  • The Harappan Civilization declined around 1900–1500 BCE, coinciding with a wave of migration of the Aryan people from the north. Physical evidence suggests climate change caused flooding, drought, and famine.
  • The cities were abandoned, and the people moved south by around 1500–600 BCE. The civilization had already fallen by the time Cyrus II (the Great, r. c. 550-530 BCE) invaded India in 530 BCE.
  • The Harappan Civilization was first discovered in 1921–1922 when Sir John Hubert Marshall led an expedition that discovered the ancient city of Harappa.

question: The name Pahalavi Dynasty in Iran is derived from the.......... (UPSC Prelims 1979)
option_1: Language
option_2: King
option_3: Religion
option_4: River
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The name of the Pahlavi dynasty in Iran comes from the language.
  • The term Pahlavi comes from Middle Persian Pahlav (from Old Iranian Parthava), which refers to the Parthian/Arsacid cultural-linguistic tradition. Pahlavi is most familiar as the name of the Middle Persian (Pahlavi) language and script used after the Parthian and early Sasanian periods.
  • The Pahlavi dynasty ruled Iran from 1925 to 1979. The dynasty’s founder, Reza Shah Pahlavi, chose the name to strengthen his nationalist credentials.

question: At which place Gautama Buddha delivered his first sermon ? (UPSC Prelims 1980)
option_1: Sarnath
option_2: Lumbini
option_3: Bodh Gaya
option_4: Vaishali
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Gautama Buddha delivered his first sermon at Sarnath, a village near the city of Benares in Uttar Pradesh, India. The event is known as Dharma-Chakra-Pravartana, which translates to “turning of the wheel of law”.
  • Sarnath is located 10 kilometers northeast of Varanasi, near the confluence of the Ganges and Varuna rivers. The deer park in Sarnath is where the Buddha first taught the Dhamma. The park is still there today.
  • Sarnath is one of the four places of pilgrimage for Buddhists and an important religious place for Jainism.

Lumbini :

  • It is a sacred area in the Terai plains of southern Nepal. It is the birthplace of the Buddha, according to Buddhist tradition, in 623 B.C.

The site includes:

1. Shakya Tank

2. Maya Devi Temple

3. Inscription on the pillar erected by the Mauryan Emperor Asoka

  • The inscription on the pillar, which is in Brahmi, gives evidence that Ashoka, emperor of the Maurya Empire, visited the place in 3rd-century BCE and identified it as the birth-place of the Buddha.

Bodhgaya :

  • It is situated west of the Phalgu River, a tributary of the Ganges (Ganga) River. Bodh Gaya contains one of the holiest of Buddhist sites: the location where, under the sacred pipal, or Bo tree, Gautama Buddha (Prince Siddhartha) attained enlightenment and became the Buddha.

Vaishali :

  • It is a Buddhist pilgrimage site and the birthplace of Lord Mahavira. It is also known as the land of Ambapali, a great Indian courtesan.
  • Vaishali is mentioned in the Ramayana and Buddhist literature. It is said that the Buddha visited Vaishali three times, spent a long time there, and delivered his last sermon. After the Buddha’s death, Vaishali hosted the Second Buddhist Council.
  • Vaishali is also home to the Buddha Relic Stupa, a popular site for Buddhists that enshrines the Buddha’s holy ashes.

question: Who is regarded as the greatest law giver of ancient India ? (UPSC Prelims 1980)
option_1: Megasthanese
option_2: Panini
option_3: Manu
option_4: Kautilya
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • Manu is considered the greatest law giver of ancient India.
  • The Manu-smriti is a text attributed to Manu, the legendary first man and lawgiver. The text is believed to date back to around 100 CE. It is considered the most authoritative book of the Hindu code Dharmashastra.
  • The Manu-smriti prescribes dharma to Hindus, which is a set of obligations for each member of the four social classes and four stages of life.
  • It is also believed to have had a significant impact on the development of Indian society and culture.

Megasthenes (c. 350–290 BC):

  • He was a Greek historian, diplomat, and ethnographer who wrote extensively about India during the reign of Chandragupta Maurya. He was the first person to describe ancient India, and for that reason he has been called “the father of Indian history”.
  • Megasthenes’ book, Indica, was lost over time, but it has been reconstructed to a certain extent using literary sources from later authors.
  • The contents of the reconstructed text include:
  • Geography, History, Fauna, Economy, Food and clothing, Society, Philosophy, Administration.
  • Megasthenes visited Pataliputra sometime during the reign of Chandragupta Maurya, and he appears to have passed through the Punjab region in north-western India. He provides a detailed account of the rivers in this area.

Panini (520 BC - 460 BC) :

  • He was a Sanskrit grammarian, logician, and philologist in ancient India. He is considered the founder of Sanskrit and its literature.
  • Panini is credited with creating a scientific theory of phonetics, phonology, and morphology. He also developed the structure of Sanskrit using almost 4,000 sutras, or rules expressed as aphorisms. The structure of the language remained largely unchanged for the next two thousand years.
  • Panini is also known for writing the ancient text Ashtadhyayi, which means “Eight Chapters”. The text was written more than 2,000 years ago and set the standard for how Sanskrit was to be written and spoken.
  • Ashtadhyayi contains a grammar problem that has been called Sanskrit’s biggest puzzle. The problem is that two or more grammar rules can apply at the same time.

Kautilya :

  • He is also known as Chanakya or Vishnugupta, was a statesman, philosopher, and advisor to the Indian king Chandragupta Maurya (c. 317-293 BCE.).
  • Kautilya was a professor of Political Science at the Gurukul (university) of Takshashila (now in Afghanistan) and later became the Prime Minister of the Mauryan Empire.
  • Kautilya is credited with writing the treatise on statecraft called the Arthashastra, which translates to “science of politics”. The Arthashastra is a 15-chapter treatise that helps a king in “the acquisition and protection of the earth”.

Kautilya’s philosophy of war included:

1. Warfare as a science

2. Attacking weakness

3. Using violence

  • Kautilya is sometimes referred to as the Indian Machiavelli because of his shrewd techniques and policies.

question: Idol worship was started in.......... (UPSC Prelims 1980)
option_1: Gupta period
option_2: Vedic period
option_3: Epic period
option_4: Maurya period
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • Idol worship began in the Epic period. It is considered a central part of ancient times and plays an important role in Hinduism.

The Epic period :

  •  The Epic Period refers to an era in ancient India, generally spanning from 1000 BCE to 400 CE, during which the great Indian epics, the Ramayana and the Mahabharata, were composed and their narratives became prevalent.

The Gupta period :

  • It was a time of cultural and scientific advancements in India from 300–600 CE. The Gupta Empire was a Hindu empire that existed from the early 4th century to the early 6th century CE. During its peak, from 319–467 CE, the empire covered much of the Indian subcontinent.
  • The Gupta period is known as the “Golden Age” of India because of its emphasis on classical Indian art and literature, and its advances in science.

The period saw:

1. Advances in poetry, prose, and drama

2. Important discoveries in mathematics and astronomy

3. Some of the most impressive examples of deified Buddhas

4. Prolific production of Hindu and Jain sacred imagery

  • The Gupta Empire was founded by Maharaja Sri Gupta and expanded through conquest and political alliances until 395 CE.
  • The capital of the Gupta dynasty was Pataliputra, an ancient city now known as Patna. The city’s central location made it easy for the empire to trade and expand.
  • The Gupta dynasty was defeated by the invading White Huns, also known as the Hephthalites. Although the dynasty was not completely destroyed, the White Huns caused considerable damage to the empire, leading to its decline.

The Vedic Age :

  • It is the period between 1500 BC and 600 BC, and is named after the Vedas, which were composed during this time. The Vedas are the primary source of information about this era.

The Vedic Age is divided into two periods:

1. Early Vedic Period or Rig Vedic Society: (1500 BC - 1000 BC)

2. Later Vedic Civilization or Painted Grey Ware Phase: (1000 BC - 600 BC)

  • The Vedic Age began with the Aryans, who were foreigners who migrated to the Indus Valley and Ganges Plain. The Aryans spoke Sanskrit and lived semi-nomadic.
  • The Vedic literature has evolved over many centuries and was passed down through generations by word of mouth.
  • The earliest surviving manuscript is from the 11th century. The Vedic literature is divided into four Vedas: Rig Veda, Sama Veda, Yajur Veda, Atharva veda.
  • The Vedic civilization was patriarchal, with the family as the fundamental unit of society.
  • The Vedic Aryans humanized many natural forces such as earth, fire, wind, rain, and thunder into gods to worship them.

The Mauryan Empire :

  • It was a powerful political and military empire in ancient India that lasted from 322 BCE to 185 BCE. It was the first pan Indian empire, covering most of India and parts of modern-day Iran.

The Mauryan Empire had three famous rulers:

1. Chandragupta Maurya: (321 - 297 BC)

2. Bindusara: (297 - 272 BC)

3. Asoka: (268 - 232 BC)

  • Chandragupta Maurya was an orphaned boy from a humble family who was trained by Kautilya, who is credited with being the master strategist behind the rise of the Mauryan Empire. Chandragupta’s son, Bindusara, succeeded him and continued to expand the empire.

The Mauryan Empire is depicted in literary sources such as:

1. Arthashastra of Kautilya

2. Indica of Megasthenes

3. Edicts issued by Ashoka

The main officials of the Mauryan Administration were:

  • Purohits, Yuvaraja, Amatya, Senapati, Adhyaksha, Sannidhatas.

question: Neolithic period of age not characterised by.......... (UPSC Prelims 1980)
option_1: Agriculture
option_2: Use of copper
option_3: Domestication of animals
option_4: Fishing
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • The Neolithic period, or New Stone Age, is not characterized by the use of copper. The Neolithic period began around 12,000 years ago and lasted until the Chalcolithic period, which began around 6,500 years ago. The Chalcolithic period saw the first use of copper, which was the first metal to be used at the end of the Neolithic Age.

The Neolithic period is characterized by:

1. Agriculture: The introduction of farming

2. Domestication of animals: The do mestication of cattle, sheep, and goats

3. Settlement: The establishment of per manent villages

4. Crafts: The appearance of pottery and weaving

5. Stone tools: Stone tools shaped by polishing or grinding

6. Subsistence: Humans no longer dependent on hunting, fishing, and gathering wild plants

  • The Neolithic period also saw the Neolithic Revolution, which included a shift from a hunter-gatherer lifestyle to one of settlement.

question: We can know about early vedic period from.......... (UPSC Prelims 1980)
option_1: Archaeological excavations
option_2: Cotemporary cultures
option_3: Rigveda
option_4: Jatak katha
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The Rigveda is the primary source of information about the early Vedic period. The Rigveda is one of the four sacred Vedas, and it was composed in 1500 BCE. The early Vedic period is the time when the Aryans first entered India, from 1500 BCE to 1000 BCE.
  • The Rigveda is the earliest literature of India and reveals information about the political organizations, state, and kingship of India during this period. It also provides information about popular assemblies and institutions responsible for administrative duties.

Archaeological excavations :

  • have also unearthed many post-Harappan/ Chalcolithic settlements from the regions of Punjab, Uttar Pradesh, and northern Rajasthan. These excavations have been conducted over the last 40 years along the Indus and Ghaggar rivers.

Contemporary cultures :

  • Cultures that existed during the same period (like Harappan remnants or other Chalcolithic cultures) can give background context, but they do not directly provide details about the early Vedic people and their lifestyle. Thus, these are secondary and indirect sources.

Jataka Katha :

  • It is a collection of stories about the previous lives of Gautama Buddha, the Buddha’s previous births as a Bodhisattva, or being who has not yet attained enlightenment. The word “Jataka” means “Birth Story” or “related to a birth” in Pali and Sanskrit.
  • The stories are often short narratives that describe how the Buddha’s soul passed from one incarnation to the next, gradually becoming stronger and more moral. The stories are preserved in all branches of Buddhism and are an important part of Buddhist art and literature.
  • The stories are often depicted on the railings and torans of stupas.
  • The stories are said to have been narrated by Gautama Buddha himself during his ministry. The number of Jataka stories is difficult to determine, but according to many scholars there are 550.

question: Name the God who lost his importance in the beginning of Christian era? (UPSC Prelims 1980)
option_1: Indra
option_2: Brahma
option_3: Vishnu
option_4: Mahesh
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Indra is the god who lost his importance at the beginning of the Christian era.
  • Indra is a Hindu god of war, rain, and thunder. He is the king of the gods and is often depicted as a warrior god. Indra is also the Indo-European cousin of the German Wotan, Norse Odin, Greek Zeus, and Roman Jupiter.
  • Indra’s importance diminished as new gods like Shiva, Vishnu, and Brahma became more important. Indra also took on new characteristics as a drunken and hedonistic god. Today, Indra is no longer widely worshipped.

question: The Indus Valley people had contacts with.......... (UPSC Prelims 1980)
option_1: Egyptians
option_2: Sumerians
option_3: Chinese
option_4: Mesopotamians
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • The Indus Valley people, also known as the Harappan Civilization, had trade relations with Mesopotamia from 3300 BCE to 1300 BCE.
  • The Indus Valley people exported gold jewelry, ivory seals and boxes, timber, cotton textiles, copper and bronze fish hooks, carnelian and precious stone beads, live chicken, shell and bone inlays, and water buffaloes.
  • The Indus Valley Civilization and Mesopotamian civilizations had an extensive maritime trade network. Harappan seals and jewelry have been found at archaeological sites in Mesopotamia, which includes most of modern-day Iraq, Kuwait, and parts of Syria.
  • The Mesopotamians referred to the Indus Valley Civilization as “Meluhha”. Meluhha was also the Sumerian name for a prominent trading partner of Sumer during the Middle Bronze Age.
  • The Indus Valley people lived on the banks of the Indus river, the longest river in Pakistan. The Indus river begins high up in the Himalayan mountains and flows nearly 3,000 kilometers to the Arabian Sea.

question: Upanishads are.......... (UPSC Prelims 1980)
option_1: Religious books of Hindus
option_2: Book dealing with ancient Hindu Laws
option_3: Books on social behaviour of man
option_4: Prayers to God
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The Upanishads are a collection of religious texts in Hinduism. They are considered the most important religious texts in Hinduism and are the foundation of Vedanta, one of the six major philosophical schools of Hinduism.
  • The Upanishads are late Vedic and post Vedic Sanskrit texts that were written between 800 BCE and 500 BCE. They are the last component of the Vedas, the sacred scriptures of most Hindu traditions.
  • The Upanishads are esoteric and philosophically oriented texts that marked the beginning of philosophy and mysticism in Indian religious history. They emphasize knowledge of the ultimate identity of all phenomena.
  • The Upanishads are religious and philosophical treatises that document the transition from the archaic ritualism of the Veda into new religious ideas and institutions. They represent the knowledge of Brahman and played a key role in the development of spiritual ideas in ancient India.
  • The Upanishads contain conversations between teachers and students, and the ideas are presented using simple dialogues.

question: Lothal is connected as excavation site of the civilisation of.......... (UPSC Prelims 1980)
option_1: Indus Valley
option_2: Sumirians
option_3: Mesopotamians
option_4: Vedic Aryan
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Lothal is an excavated site of the Indus Valley Civilization and is the only port town of the civilization. The Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) discovered Lothal in 1954 and excavated the site from 1955 1960.
  • Lothal is located in the Bhal region of Gujarat, India, and is believed to have been constructed around 2200 BCE.
  • The site is notable for the discovery of several ruins of the Indus Valley Civilization, including a planned township with a drainage system and a cylindrical seal with Harappan characters and an alligator symbol.
  • In April 2014, Lothal was nominated as a UNESCO World Heritage Site, and its application is pending on the tentative list.

question: In which way Sarnath is associated with Lord Buddha ? (UPSC Prelims 1980)
option_1: He resided there
option_2: He was born there
option_3: He ruled there
option_4: He preached his first Sermon there
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • Sarnath is associated with Lord Buddha because he preached his first sermon there. The sermon, known as the Dharmachakra Pravartana Sutta or the “Turning of the Wheel of Dharma”, took place shortly after Buddha attained enlightenment.
  • Sarnath is an ancient Buddhist site in Varanasi, Uttar Pradesh. The Sarnath Buddhist Temple is a large complex with a museum, shrines, and an archaeological excavation.

question: Which of the following is the most important cause for the decline of Buddhism after Ashoka? (UPSC Prelims 1980)
option_1: Non patronage by the kings
option_2: Condemnation of animal sacrifices
option_3: Growth of licentious practices in Buddhist centers
option_4: Allegiance to the middle path
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The most important cause for the decline of Buddhism after Ashoka was the lack of royal patronage.
  • Buddhism’s royal sponsorship dwindled over time. After Ashoka, Kaniska, and Harsavardhan, no notable kings supported Buddhism. The growth of any faith is aided by royal sponsorship.
  • The decline of Buddhism in the Indian subcontinent was a gradual process that ended around the 12th century.

Other causes for the decline of Buddhism include:

Decline of Buddhist Sanghas:

1. Due to corruption and luxury oriented monks

2. Revival and reform of Vedic Brahmanism

3. Emergence of multiple sects in Buddhism

4. Use of Sanskrit by later Buddhist monks

5. Language : Most Kings and Poets wrote in Classical Sanskrit language, which is the language of Brahman’s, and language like Pali was totally left ignored.

question: Which of the following is not related to the Gandhara School of Art ? (UPSC Prelims 1980)
option_1: Ellora
option_2: Ajanta
option_3: Khajuraho
option_4: Elephanta
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • Khajuraho is not related to the Gandhara School of Art.
  • The Gandhara art style is known for its first human representations of the Buddha. It is primarily Mahayana and has Greco Roman influences. The Ellora, Ajanta, and Elephanta caves all have influences from the Gandhara school of art.

The Ellora Caves :

  • These are a multi-religious rock-cut cave complex in the Aurangabad District of Maharashtra, India. The caves are located in the Charanandri hills, about 1,500 years ago, during the Rashtrakuta dynasty. The caves contain 243 caves with inscriptions dating from the 6th century CE onwards.
  • They showcase the spirit of tolerance and religious tolerance through the architectural activities carried out by the followers of three prominent religions:
  1. Buddhism
  2. Brahmanism and
  3. Jainism.
  • The caves are maintained by the Archaeological Survey of India (ASI) and were declared a World Heritage Site in 1983.

The Ajanta Caves :

  • These are a UNESCO World Heritage Site in the Aurangabad district of Maharashtra, India. They are a collection of 29 rock-cut Buddhist cave monuments dating from the 2nd century BCE to about 480 CE.
  • The caves are a prime example of Buddhist rock-cut architecture, and feature art, architecture, paintings, and socio-cultural, religious, and political history. The caves are cut into a horse shoe shaped bend of rock surface, nearly 76 meters in height, overlooking a narrow stream called Waghora. The caves were discovered in 1819 by British soldiers and have influenced many artists and styles.

The Elephanta Caves :

  • These are a collection of 7 rock-cut cave temples on Elephanta Island in Mumbai Harbor, India. The caves were built between the 5th and 6th centuries AD by the Rashtrakuta rulers. The caves are mainly dedicated to the Hindu god Shiva and feature images from Hindu mythology. The caves are a UNESCO World Heritage Site and are considered one of the most perfect examples of Indian art.

question: Which of the following was the most important characteristic of Kanishka’s rule ? (UPSC Prelims 1980)
option_1: Expansion of Buddhism outside India
option_2: Re-emergence of Jainism
option_3: Fourth Buddhist Council at Srinagar
option_4: Gandhara School of Art
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The most important characteristic of Kanishka’s rule was the Fourth Buddhist Council at Srinagar.
  • The Kushan period saw a resurgence of Buddhism in Kashmir, especially during the reign of Kanishka. Kanishka was a convert to Buddhism and a great patron of the religion. In 72 AD, he sponsored the Fourth Buddhist Council at Kundalvana, Kashmir.
  • The council refined the teachings of Mahayana Buddhism and divided Buddhism into two sects:

1) Mahayana

2) Hinayana

  • Kanishka was also a patron of art and architecture. His coins feature a mix of Indian, Greek, and Zoroastrian deities.

The Gandhara School of Art :

  • It is a style of Buddhist art that developed in the Gandhara region between the 1st century BCE and the 7th century CE. The region is now located in northwestern Pakistan and eastern Afghanistan.
  • The Gandhara School of Art is also known as the Indo-Greek art because of its heavy influence of Greek or Hellenistic sculpture. It is a unique blending of Indian, Greek, and Roman art styles. The main characteristics of the Gandhara School of Art are the realistic depictions of humans and animals, as well as the use of color.
  • The Gandhara School of Art flourished primarily in Afghanistan and present day North-Western India. Some of the most prominent locations include Taxila, Peshawar, Begram, and Bamiyan.
  • The Bamiyan Buddha represents the Gandhara School of art. The Bamiyan valley, 230 km northwest of Kabul separates the Hindu Kush from the Koh-i-Baba mountains.

question: Saka era commenced from.......... (UPSC Prelims 1980)
option_1: 78 AD
option_2: 120 AD
option_3: 1000 AD
option_4: 1953 AD
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The Saka era, also known as the Salivāhana era, began in 78 AD.
  • The era is used in India, Indochina, and Indonesia, and is also used by the Indian government alongside the Gregorian calendar.
  • The Saka era may have been started by the Shakas to commemorate Kanishka’s accession to the throne in 78 AD. Kanishka was the emperor of the Kushan dynasty in the second century, and is known for his military, political, and spiritual achievements.
  • The Saka era was adopted as the era of the Indian national calendar in 1957. The official Shaka calendar year is tied to the Gregorian date of March 22 every year, except in Gregorian leap years when it starts on March 21.

The months of the Saka calendar are :

  • Chaitra, Vaishakha, Jyeshtha, Ashadha, Shravana, Bhadra, Ashwin, Kartika, Agrahayana, Pausha, Magha, Phalguna.

question: Earliest Indian coins belonged to which peroid? (UPSC Prelims 1981)
option_1: 5th century BC
option_2: Ashok’s reign
option_3: 1st century AD
option_4: 3rd century AD
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The earliest Indian coins were minted around the 5th century BC. These coins were made of copper and silver, and were issued by imperial dynasties and middle kingdoms. The earliest coins were called punch-marked coins, and were first used in eastern Uttar Pradesh and Bihar.
  • The first coins were globules of electrum, a naturally occurring alloy of gold and silver. These coins were crude, but had a definite weight and were stamped with incuse punches. They were issued by local authorities around 650 BC.
  • The Saurashtra Janapada coins, which date from 450 to 300 BCE, are considered to be the earliest die-struck figurative coins from ancient India. These coins are also thought to be the earliest source of Hindu representational forms.

question: Meghasthenes visited the court of................ (UPSC Prelims 1981)
option_1: 5th century BC
option_2: Ashok’s reign
option_3: 1st century AD
option_4: 3rd century AD
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • Megasthenes was a Greek ambassador sent by Seleucus I Nicator (the founder of the Seleucid Empire, successor of Alexander’s territories) to the court of Chandragupta Maurya, the founder of the Maurya Empire.
  • Megasthenes visited Pataliputra during the reign of Chandragupta Maurya. He was impressed by the kingdom’s size, the Mauryan palaces, and Chandragupta’s administration. Megasthenes’ book Indika describes the Mauryan reign and it was introduced to the West.

question: Lothal is ......... (UPSC Prelims 1982)
option_1: A closed water system
option_2: A poisonous gas
option_3: A place in Gujarat where excavations have established a link with the Indus Valley civilisation
option_4: Legal action taken against defectors
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • Lothal is a place in Gujarat where excavations have established a link with the Indus Valley civilization.
  • Lothal is one of the southernmost sites of the Indus Valley civilization, located in the Bhāl region of what is now the state of Gujarat. The port city is believed to have been built in 2,200 BC.
  • Lothal was discovered in 1954 and excavations have revealed the world’soldest known artificial dock. The dock was connected to an old course of the Sabarmati River. Other features include the acropolis, the lower town, the bead factory, the warehouses, and the drainage system.
  • Lothal is a metropolis with an upper and a lower town. On its northern side is a basin with a vertical wall, inlet and outlet channels which has been identified as a tidal dockyard.
  • The people of Lothal made significant contributions to human civilization in the Indus era, in city planning, art, architecture, science, pottery, etc.

question: Out of the following the oldest dynasty is.......... (UPSC Prelims 1982)
option_1: Mauryas
option_2: Nandas
option_3: Pallavas
option_4: Guptas
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • The Nanda dynasty is the oldest family that ruled Magadha in northern India between 343 and 321 BCE.

The Mauryan Empire :

  • It was a historical power in South Asia that existed from 322–185 BCE. It was the first pan-Indian empire, covering most of India and parts of modern-day Iran. The empire’s capital was Pataliputra and it was centralized by the conquest of the Indo Gangetic Plain.

The Mauryan Empire had three famous rulers:

1. Chandragupta Maurya: (321 - 297 BC)

2. Bindusara: (297 - 272 BC)

3. Asoka: (268 - 232 BC)

  • Chandragupta Maurya, an orphaned boy from a humble family, is credited with being the master strategist behind the rise of the Mauryan Empire. He defeated the remaining Macedonian satrapies in the northwest of the Indian subcontinent by 317 BCE. He also defeated an invasion ledby Seleucus I, a Macedonian general from Alexander’s army, and gained additional territory west of the Indus River.
  • The Mauryan Empire declined in 185 BC when the last king, Brihadratha, was killed by his own commander-in-chief, Pushyamitra. Pushyamitra later established the Shunga dynasty and mostly ruled in the central part of India.

The Nanda dynasty :

  • It ruled Magadha in northern India from around 343 to 321 BCE. The dynasty is known for its well-organized administration and large armies. However, the Nandas also imposed high taxes to collect more wealth, which led to dislike for them amongst the masses.

Here are some details about the Nanda dynasty:

1. The Nanda dynasty was the fifth ruling dynasty of Magadha in northern India during the fourth century BCE.

2. The Nanda dynasty began in 385 BC with the coronation of Mahapadma Nanda in the capital of Pataliputra.

3. The Nandas overthrew the Shaishunaga dynasty, and expanded the empire to include a larger part of northern India.

4. The Nandas are prominent in Indian history because of their well organized administrative structure.

5. The Nanda monarchs collected taxes systematically and prioritized them.

6. The Nandas are portrayed as extremely rich and as ruthless collectors of various kinds of taxes.

7. In Alexander’s period, Nanda military robustness was estimated at 20,000 Horse Guards, 200,000 infantry, 2,000 quadriga (chariots), and 3,000 elephants.

8. The final Nanda ruler was Dhanananda.

9. When the forces of Chandragupta besieged Pataliputra in around 20 B.C, the army of Nandas got overpowered.

Pallava Dynasty :

  • Simhavishnu is considered the founder of the Pallava dynasty, which ruled from 275–897 CE. Simhavishnu’s reign began in the 6th century CE, and many later rulers traced their lineage back to him.
  • Simhavishnu was a successful conqueror and administrator who established his capital at Kanchi, also known as Kanchipuram. He was also known as Avanisimha, son of Simhavarman III.
  • The Pallava dynasty ruled the southern regions of India. They made significant contributions to religion, philosophy, art, and coins. Many Pallava royal inscriptions were in Sanskrit or Prakrit, which were considered the official languages.

question: Saka era wa introduced by.......... (UPSC Prelims 1982)
option_1: Ashoka
option_2: Kanishka
option_3: Chandragupta Maurya
option_4: Harsha Vardhan
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • Kanishka, the emperor of the Kushan Dynasty, is believed to have started the Saka era in 78 AD. The Saka era is still used in parts of India and Pakistan, and is also used by the Indian government calendar.
  • The Saka era marked the beginning of the Saka Samvat, a Hindu calendar that was introduced as the “Indian National Calendar” in 1957.
  • The Saka calendar is a lunisolar calendar, which means it considers both the phases of the moon and the solar year. It is used alongside the Gregorian calendar by The Gazette of India and in news broadcasts by All India Radio.

question: The script of Indus Valley civilisation was.......... (UPSC Prelims 1982)
option_1: Persian
option_2: Dravidian
option_3: Sanskrit
option_4: Undeciphered
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • The script of the Indus Valley Civilization is undeciphered. The Indus Valley Civilization was a Bronze Age civilization that lasted from 3300 BC to 1300 BC.
  • The Indus script is the earliest form of writing known in the Indian subcontinent. The Indus script is a right-to-left script that may date back to 3500–1900 BCE. Some say the script is pictorial, alphabetic, and phonetic. Others believe the script is made up of 400 signs, with 39 being elementary signs and the rest being variations.
  • Some scholars believe the Indus script is related to the Dravidian or Elamo Dravidian language family. Speakers of Hindi, Bengali, and other Neo-Indo-Aryan languages have also interpreted the Indus texts as Sanskrit.
  • Despite being undeciphered, the Indus script is important because it is the only evidence that the Indus Valley civilization existed.

question: Which of the following statements is not correct about Nalanda University ? (UPSC Prelims 1983)
option_1: It was an ancient Buddhist learning centre
option_2: A Chinese Pilgrim Hiuen Tsang studied here
option_3: We came to know about it only through the writing of Chinese pilgrims
option_4: It was mainly responsible for spreading Buddhism to China, Japan and South East Asia
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The statement that is not correct about Nalanda University is “We came to know about it only through the writing of Chinese pilgrims”.
  • Nalanda University was a large Buddhist monastery and center of learning from 5th century CE to 1200 CE. It was a residential university with 2,000 teachers and 10,000 students. Students from all over Asia, including China, Persia, Japan, Korea, Sri Lanka, Turkey, and Greater India, came to study at Nalanda.
  • The university was founded by Kumaragupta I, son of Chandragupta II. His successors later expanded the university by building additional monasteries and temples.

question: The philosophical systems of the Hindus were propounded and properly codified in the.......... (UPSC Prelims 1983)
option_1: Vedic age
option_2: Maurya age
option_3: Kanishka age
option_4: Gupta age
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The Vedic age, which lasted from 1500 BCE to 500 BCE, is when the philosophical systems of the Hindus were propounded and codified.
  • The Vedic age is a period in Indian history when the Vedas were composed, which are the oldest sacred texts of Hinduism.
  • The Vedas are liturgical texts that form the basis of the Brahmanical ideology. The Vedas are the earliest philosophical texts in India and include the Rigveda, the Samaveda, the Yajurveda, and the Atharvaveda.

Hindu philosophy includes six systems:

1. Sankhya

2. Yoga

3. Nyaya

4. Vaisheshika

5. Mimamsa

6. Vedanta

  • Most of these schools of thought believe in the theory of Karma and rebirth.

The Maurya Empire :

  • It was a dynasty in ancient India thatlasted from 322–185 BCE. It was the first pan-Indian empire, covering most of the Indian region, including parts of modern-day Iran. The Maurya Empire was one of the most powerful empires in the Indian subcontinent’s history, and this era was marked by high levels of cultural development and economic prosperity.

The Maurya Empire had three famous rulers:

1. Chandragupta Maurya: (324/321 297 BC)

2. Bindusara: (297–272 BC)

3. Asoka: (268–232 BC)

  • The Maurya Empire was succeeded by the Sunga Dynasty, which was established by Pushyamitra Sunga in 185 BC.

question:
The following were famous scholars in ancient India.......... (UPSC Prelims 1983)

1. Ashvaghosh

2. Nagarjuna

3. Vasumitra

4. Buddhaghosa

Which of them was/were contemporary of Kanishka?
option_1: 1, 2, 3, 4
option_2: 1, 3 and 4
option_3: 1 and 2 only
option_4: 1, 2 and 3
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

Ashvaghosha (80–150 CE) :

  • He was a Buddhist philosopher, poet, dramatist, musician, and orator from India. He is considered the first Sanskrit dramatist and the greatest Indian poet before Kālidāsa. He is also credited with popularizing the kavya style of Sanskrit poetry.
  • Ashvaghosha was born in Saketa, which is now known as Ayodhya. He was a Brahmin who later converted to Buddhism. His work is said to rival the contemporary Ramayana, and he wrote in Classical Sanskrit, unlike much of the Buddhist literature before him.

Nagarjuna :

  • He was a contemporary of Satavahana king Gautmiputra and the author of the fundamental text of the Brahminical Logicians, Gautama Aksapada. He was also a contemporary of Kanishka, and is considered one of the most important figures in the development of Mahayana Buddhist philosophy.
  • Nagarjuna is known as the “Einstein of India” because he promoted the Sunyavada theory, which is similar to Einstein’s theory of relativity.
  • Nagarjuna was a Buddhist monk who lived between 150 and 250 CE, most likely in southern India. He was born into an upper caste Brahmin family, most likely in the southern Andhra area of India. Nagarjuna was also a contemporary of Asvaghosha and Vasumitra, who were also scholars in the court of Kanishka.

Vasumitra :

  • He was a Buddhist monk and philosopher who lived in the 2nd century CE. He was a member of the Sarvastivada school and presided over the 4th Buddhist council in Kashmir. Vasumitra is credited with contributing to the Mahavibhasa, a classic Buddhist text and encyclopedia of Buddhist philosophy.
  • Vasumitra is also known for his theory that temporal predicates are determined by the function of a dharma.

Buddhaghosa :

  • He was a 5th-century Buddhist scholar, philosopher, and commentator from India. He worked at the Great Monastery in Anurādhapura, Sri Lanka, and was a member of the Theravada school.
  • Buddhaghosa’s name means “voice of Buddha”. He is known for his Visuddhimagga, which means “The Path of Purification”.
  • Buddhaghosa is a paradigmatic saint scholar in the Sri Lankan Theravāda Mahavihara tradition. He is considered one of the most important commentators of the Theravada.

question: Ancient Indian art and architecture reached its zenith during the period of.......... (UPSC Prelims 1983)
option_1: Rajputs
option_2: Mauryas
option_3: Guptas
option_4: None of these
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The Gupta Empire (320–550 CE) is considered a golden age for ancient Indian art and architecture. The Gupta period saw many achievements in the arts, including the construction of many temples.
  • The temples at Deogarh, Sanchi, and Bhittargaon are famous examples. The 29 cave temples at Ajanta and Bagh were also built during this period.
  • The Gupta period is also known as the “Golden Period of Indian Architecture”. The later Gupta rulers, who were Hindus, sponsored the construction of many temples.
  • The Maurya and Gupta periods are also considered to have seen the peak of Indian architecture. The stone pillar erected by Emperor Ashoka is an example of Indian sculpture.
  • The stupa at Sanchi and cave sculptures at Karla, Nashik, Ajanta, and Ellora are other examples.

question: Major difference between “varna” and “Jati” is that.......... (UPSC Prelims 1984)
option_1: Jatis are limited whereas varnas are unlimited
option_2: Jatis are only four whereas varnas are many
option_3: Varnas are only four whreas Jatis are many
option_4: Jatis is decided by birth but not Varna
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The main difference between “varna” and “jati” is that “varna” is based on occupation, while “jati” is based on birth.
  • “Varna” comes from the word “Vri”, which means “the choice of one’s occupation”. “Varna” is a division of society based on occupation. In Hinduism, society is traditionally divided into four classes or castes, called “varnas”.
  • “Jati” comes from the word “Jana”, which means “taking birth”. “Jati” is a division based on birth units of Hindu society. There are only four “varnas” but there are thousands of “jatis”.

question: Rigved the oldest of the vedas chiefly contains...........  (UPSC Prelims 1984)
option_1: Collection of hymns to gods
option_2: Methods of vedic yaganas
option_3: Ealry Aryan culture
option_4: Origin and works of Hindu gods
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The Rigveda is the oldest of the four Vedas and is a collection of hymns and prayers. It contains 10 mandalas and 1,028 hymns. The hymns are in the form of dialogues and are chanted in various rituals.
  • The hymns are in praise of various gods and goddesses, such as the god of fire, god of rain, and Lord Vishnu.
  • The Rigveda is one of the most important texts of the Hindu tradition. It is composed of the Samhita, Brahmanas, Aranyakas, and Upanishads. The Rigveda Samhita is the core text and is a collection of 10 books with 1,028 hymns in about 10,600 verses.
  • The Rigveda is the oldest known Vedic Sanskrit text, with its early layers among the oldest extant texts in any Indo-European language. The sounds and texts of the Rigveda have been orally transmitted since the 2nd millennium BCE.

question: Which of the following presents the most significiant features of Indus Valley Civilisation?  (UPSC Prelims 1984)
option_1: Buildings with perfect arches
option_2: Use of burnt mud bricks
option_3: Use of sun dried mud bricks
option_4: None of these
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • The most significant feature of the Indus Valley Civilization is the use of burnt brick buildings.
  • The Indus Valley Civilization, also known as the Harappan Civilization, is credited with pioneering the use of fired bricks in construction.
  • The bricks were uniformly sized and shaped, which allowed for precise construction. The Great Bath at Mohenjo-Daro is an example of brick architecture.

Other significant features of the Indus Valley Civilization include:

  1. Systematic town planning
  2. Underground drainage system
  3. Fortified citadel
  4. Personal cleanliness
  5. Ceramics
  6. Casting
  7. Forging of metals
  8. Manufacturing of cotton and woolen textiles
  • The Indus Valley Civilization flourished around 2500 BC in Pakistan and Western India.

question: Ajanta paintings depict......... (UPSC Prelims 1984)
option_1: Ramayana
option_2: Mahabharata
option_3: Jatakas
option_4: Panchatantra
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The paintings in the Ajanta caves in Maharashtra, India depict the Jataka tales. T he Jataka tales are Buddhist legends that tell the stories of the Buddha’s previous lives.
  • The paintings also depict other incidents from the Buddha’s life, as well as contemporary events and social life.
  • These narratives include stories of the Buddha taking birth in both human and animal forms. The fables also contain ancient morals and cultural lores that are also found in Hindu and Jain texts.

The Ramayana :

  • It is a sacred Hindu epic that tells the story of Rama, an incarnation of the god Vishnu. It is one of the two important epics of Hinduism, along with the Mahabharata.
  • The Ramayana is a popular epic in South and Southeast Asia, and is told and retold in many ways, including: Orally, Literature, Comic books, Plays, Movies.
  • The Ramayana is composed in Sanskrit, probably not before 300 BCE, by the poet Valmiki. In its present form, it consists of some 24,000 couplets divided into seven books.
  • The story takes place in the city of Ayodhya, the capital of Kosala. It tells the story of King Rama, who must save his kidnapped wife, Sita. The story begins with the king, Dasaratha, growing older and noticing omens that suggest his end is near. He decides to step down and make his son Rama king.

The Mahabharata :

  • It is a Sanskrit epic poem and sacred text for Hindus. It is one of two major Smriti texts and Sanskrit epics of ancient India, the other being the Ramayana. The title translates to “Great Epic of the Bharata Dynasty”.
  • The Mahabharata is about the struggle between two groups of cousins, the Pandavas and the Kauravas, to gain supreme power and rule the kingdom.

The story revolves around the following themes:

1. Dharma: Believing in one’s sacred duty

2. Right actions: Taking actions to follow the right path of dharma

3. Eliminating evil: Eliminating evil actions from one’s life

The Panchatantra :

  • It is a collection of 84 animal fables and stories written in Sanskrit verse and prose. The stories are arranged within a frame story, meaning each tale contributes to a larger narrative. The Panchatantra is thought to have been written by Vishnu Sharma over 2,500 years ago. The surviving work is dated to around 200 BCE, but the fables are likely much older.

question: Indus Valley Civilisation was spread over..........  (UPSC Prelims 1984)
option_1: Baluchistan, Sind, Punjab and Rajasthan
option_2: Sind,Punjab and Kashmir
option_3: Afghanistan, Sind, Punjab, Rajasthan, Gujarat and Kashmir
option_4: None of these
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The Indus Valley Civilization was spread over Baluchistan, Sind, Punjab, and Rajasthan. It also extended from northeastern Afghanistan in the north to Gujarat state in the south, and from Balochistan in the west to western Uttar Pradesh in the east.
  • The Indus Valley Civilization is considered one of the three most ancient civilizations, and its geographical extent was the most extensive of the three.
  • The largest number of sites from this civilization are in the Punjab region, Gujarat, Haryana, Rajasthan, Uttar Pradesh, Jammu and Kashmir states, Sindh, and Balochistan.
  • The Indus Valley Civilization’s two great cities, Harappa and Mohenjo-Daro, emerged around 2600 BCE along the Indus River Valley in the Sindh and Punjab provinces of Pakistan.
  • Excavations of Mohen-Jo-Daro have provided insight into the city life of this civilization, which has been described as having a distinct identity and culture.

question: What was the main feature of Maurya dynasty in India?  (UPSC Prelims 1984)
option_1: Education was widespread
option_2: Terrorists were driven out of Sind and Punjab
option_3: Removel of land tax for the first time
option_4: Decentralisation of administration
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • The main feature of the Maurya dynasty in India was the decentralization of its administration. The Mauryan government was highly centralized, with the emperor as the supreme power and source of all authority.
  • However, the emperor was not a complete autocrat, and his authority was assisted by a Council of Ministers called “Mantriparishad”.
  • The empire also had a powerful military, a single currency, and a network of regional governors and administrators. The Maurya Empire was founded in 322 BC by Chandragupta Maurya.
  • The empire’s borders extended from the Hindukush mountains to the Great Indian Desert, Gujarat, the Indus and the Ganges valley, and more.
  • The empire also had a civil service to provide justice and security for merchants, farmers, and traders.

question: Main preachings of Buddha were in regard to.......... (UPSC Prelims 1984)
option_1: Right faith and conduct
option_2: Love of god
option_3: Practice of rituals
option_4: Idol worship
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Buddha’s teachings are known as “dharma”. They emphasize the importance of moral conduct, which includes:
  • Right speech, Right action, Right livelihood.
  • Buddha’s teachings are guided by principles of compassion, respect, and consideration for others.

Buddha’s moral code, or precepts, include: 

  1. Not taking the life of anything living
  2. Not taking anything not freely given
  3. Abstaining from sexual misconduct and sensual overindulgence
  4. Refraining from untrue speech
  5. Avoiding intoxication

Buddha’s teachings also include:

  • Loving, kindness, Compassion, Appreciative joy, Equanimity.
  • Buddha deemphasized Brahmanical rituals by making karma an ethical act and focusing on intention. He also discouraged idol worship, saying that people go to refuge to the mountain, to the trees, to the pagodas and they pay homage to these things because of fear and threatened with fear.

The Eightfold Path is a set of teachings in Buddhism that include:

  • Right View, Right Speech, Right Intention, Right Concentration, Right Mindfulness, Right Efforts, Right Action, Right Livelihood.
  • To attain enlightenment and escape suffering, one must practice these habits.

question: Gautam buddha attained Nirvan at.......... (UPSC Prelims 1985)
option_1: Bodh Gaya
option_2: Sarnath
option_3: Kushi Nagar
option_4: Lumbini
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Buddha attained Nirvana under a peepal tree at Bodh Gaya. He also attained Mahaparinirvana in Kushinagar, which is in present-day Uttar Pradesh.
  • Mahaparinirvana is the ultimate state of Nirvana, which is everlasting, highest peace, and happiness. Kushinagar is the f inal resting place of Gautam Buddha.

Sarnath :

  • Gautama Buddha delivered his first sermon at Sarnath, a village near the city of Benares in Uttar Pradesh, India. The event is known as Dharma-Chakra-Pravartana, which translates to “turning of the wheel of law”.
  • Sarnath is located 10 kilometers northeast of Varanasi, near the confluence of the Ganges and Varuna rivers. The deer park in Sarnath is where the Buddha first taught the Dhamma. The park is still there today.
  • Sarnath is one of the four places of pilgrimage for Buddhists and an important religious place for Jainism.

Lumbini :

  • It is a sacred area in the Terai plains of southern Nepal. It is the birthplace of the Buddha, according to Buddhist tradition, in 623 B.C.

The site includes:

  1. Shakya Tank
  2. Maya Devi Temple
  3. Inscription on the pillar erected by the Mauryan Emperor Asoka
  • The inscription on the pillar, which is in Brahmi, gives evidence that Ashoka, emperor of the Maurya Empire, visited the place in 3rd-century BCE and identified it as the birth-place of the Buddha.

Kushinagar :

  • It is a town in the Kushinagar district in Uttar Pradesh, India. Located 53 kilometres (33 miles) east of Gorakhpur on National Highway 27, Kushinagar is an important and popular Buddhist pilgrimage site, where Buddhists believe Gautama Buddha attained parinirvana.

question: Which of the following statements is incorrect ?  (UPSC Prelims 1985)
option_1: Chandragupta Vikramaditya killed the saka
option_2: Samudragupta is known as Indian Napolean
option_3: Skandagupta defeated Huns
option_4: Kumaragupta restord the embankment of Sundarsana Lake
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The incorrect statement is “Chandragupta Vikramaditya killed the Saka”.
  • Chandragupta II, also known as Vikramaditya, was the third ruler of the Gupta Empire in India. He defeated the Sakas, who were Iranian by origin, and assumed the title of Sakari, which means “destroyer of the Sakas”. He also annexed three Satrapa kingdoms and conquered the territories of Malwa, Gujarat, and Kathiawar.
  • Chandragupta II was also the first Gupta ruler to issue silver coins called Rupaka. He was known as an ideal king for his generosity, courage, and patronage to scholars.

question: Which of the following can be regarded as the chief contribution of Buddhism to India?  (UPSC Prelims 1985)
option_1: Art and literature
option_2: Architecture
option_3: Ahimsa and self purification
option_4: Rituals
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • Buddhism’s chief contribution to India is the concept of ahimsa, which later became a cherished value of the nation. Ahimsa is a Sanskrit word that means “non-injury”.
  • Buddhism also contributed to India’s art and architecture, including the stupas at Sanchi, Bharhut, and Gaya.
  • Buddhism also promoted a sense of national feeling among Indians. It emphasized virtues like charity, purity, compassion, self-sacrifice, and truthfulness.
  • Buddhism’s architecture developed in the Indian subcontinent in the third century BCE. The three main elements of Buddhist architecture are stupas, chaityas, and viharas.
  • Buddhism rejects the caste system and the religious authority of the Brahmins. Buddhism believes that all people are created equal, and that caste is not a punishment for deeds committed in a past life.
  • Buddhism also rejects the formal rituals of Hinduism. It challenges Hindu traditions by not being interested in abstract speculation about the creation of the world or the existence of gods.
  • Buddhism’s inclusiveness and humanism has always stood in contrast to Brahmanical practices of birth-based segregation and bodily violence.
  • Buddhism and Hinduism agree on karma, dharma, moksha, and reincarnation.

question:
Match the columns :    (UPSC Prelims 1985)                                         

A.Upanishads

1. 

World’s oldest scriptures

B. Samveda

2. 

Magical chants

C. Rigveda

3.

Mysterious doctrine

D.Atharvaveda

4. 

Ritual

Select the correct answer from the codes given below :

 A B C D
option_1: 4 3 2 1
option_2: 3 4 1 2
option_3: 1 2 3 4
option_4: 4 2 3 1
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

The Upanishads :

  • They are a collection of religious and philosophical texts in Hinduism. They are considered the most important religioustexts in Hinduism and are the fundamental texts of Vedanta, one of the six major philosophical schools of Hinduism.
  • The Upanishads were written between 800 BCE and 500 BCE.
  • They are late Vedic and post-Vedic Sanskrit texts that document the transition from the archaic ritualism of the Veda into new religious ideas and institutions. The Upanishads establish and explain the religion’s essential doctrines, also known as Sanatan Dharma, which means “Eternal Order” or “Eternal Path”.
  • The Upanishads are revered in Hinduism and played a key role in the development of spiritual ideas in ancient India. The Upanishad texts contained conversations between teachers and students, and the ideas were presented using simple dialogues.

There are between 180-200 Upanishads, but the best known are the 13 which are embedded in the four Vedas:

  • Rig Veda, Sama Veda, Yajur Veda, Atharva Veda.

Some of the Upanishads include:

  • Brhadaranyaka Upanishad, Chandogya Upanishad, Taittiriya Upanishad, Aitereya Upanishad, Kausitaki Upanishad, Kena Upanishad, Katha Upanishad, Isha Upanishad, Svetasvatara Upanishad, Mundaka Upanishad.

The four Vedas are:

» Rig Veda : Contains hymns about my thology

» Sama Veda : Contains hymns about re ligious rituals

» Yajur Veda : Contains instructions for religious rituals

» Atharva Veda : Contains spells against enemies, sorcerers, and diseases

  • The Vedas are written in Sanskrit, an ancient Indo-Aryan language, and their verses are composed in a poetic and rhythmic style.

Each Veda has four key parts:

1. Samhita: Poetry that is recited

2. Brahmana: Manuals on various ritu als and the ways to perform them

3. Aranyaka: Significance of various rit uals and rules for students

4. Upanishads: Abstract, higher order philosophy

  • The Sama Veda is also known as the “Veda of chants” and is believed to have been compiled around 1200 or 1000 BCE. It is the shortest and most closely connected to the Rig Veda. The Sama Veda contains a collection of melodies and chants, and is not recited or read but sung.

question: Which of the following was not known to Indus Valley people ?  (UPSC Prelims 1985)
option_1: Silver
option_2: Copper
option_3: Iron
option_4: Bronze
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • Iron was not known to the Indus Valley people. The Indus Valley Civilization, also known as the Harappan Civilization, existed from 3300–1300 BCE. This was before the Iron Age, and the Indus Valley people were unaware of iron.
  • The Indus Valley people were aware of other metals, including gold, silver, copper, bronze, and tin. They manufactured objects from gold and silver, and made heavy use of silver.

question:
Match the columns :   (UPSC Prelims 1985)

A. Amarnath

B. Rock cut      tempels

A B C D

option_4: 4 3 2 1
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • Amaranth cave is a Hindu shrine located in Jammu and Kashmir, India. The cave is situated at an altitude of 3,888 m.
  • A rock cut temple is carved from a large rock and excavated and cut to imitate a wooden or masonry temple with wall decorations and works of art. Pancha Rathas is an example of monolith Indian rock cut architecture dating from the late 7th century located at Mahabalipuram, a UNESCO World Heritage Site.

question: Lineage is...........  (UPSC Prelims 1986)
option_1: A straigth line joining any two points on the globe
option_2: The line of descendants of a particular ancestor
option_3: The paleolithic age
option_4: The linking of genes
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • Lineage is the line of descendants from a particular ancestor. For example, a sequence of myself, my father, and my grandfather is a lineage.

question: Which of the following is a characteristic feature of the Indus Valley Civilisation ? (UPSC Prelims 1986)
option_1: Their chif deities were Mother Goddess and Shiva
option_2: They worshipped bulls
option_3: They had all amenities of a developed city life
option_4: They did not know of iron
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

The Indus Valley Civilization, also known as the Harappan Civilization, had many characteristic features:

1. Drainage system: The Indus Valley Civilization had a closed drainage system with each house having its own drainage and soak pit.

2. Baked brick houses: The Indus Valley Civilization used baked bricks of equal size.

3. Urban centers: The Indus Valley Civilization developed urban centers.

4. Religion: The Indus Valley Civilization had a polytheistic religion that included worship of the Mother Goddess, a male deity, and animals.

5. Trade: The Indus Valley Civilization traded with other civilizations.

  • Other features: The Indus Valley Civilization also had:
  • Town planning, Personal cleanliness Ceramics, Casting, Forging of metals, Manufacturing of cotton and, woolen textiles, New techniques of handicrafts and metallurgy, Graineries, Ports, Seals.
  • The Indus Valley Civilization flourished around 2500 BC in Pakistan and Western India. The climate of the Indus Valley is semi-arid, watered by winter rains, seasonal streams, and human landscaping.

question: Which of the following was the principle article of food of the Indus Valley people? (UPSC Prelims 1986)
option_1: Fish
option_2: Palm date
option_3: Eggs
option_4: Wheat
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • The Indus Valley people’s main food crops were wheat and barley. They may have made these into bread or cooked them into gruel or porridge.
  • The Indus Valley people also fished for riverine, estuarine, and marine fish, as well as shellfish.
  • They were non-vegetarians, and evidence of domesticated animals is abundant. However, some pottery shows that they also used vegetable oils. A recent study found that the Indus Valley people preferred beef over other types of meat.
  • The Indus Valley people also cultivated cotton and date palms. Date palms have been a staple food of the Middle East and the Indus Valley for thousands of years.

question: Which of the following was the main reason of the downfall of Mauryan empire? (UPSC Prelims 1986)
option_1: Weak sucessors of Ashoka who could not control the empire properly
option_2: Religious policy of  Ashoka
option_3: Ashoka maintained friendly relations with his neighbours
option_4: None of the above
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

The Mauryan Empire declined due to a number of factors, including the weakness of Ashoka’s successors:

1. Weak successors :

  • Ashoka’s successors were less capable and efficient rulers, which led to administrative and military weaknesses. The succession of rulers after Ashoka was weak and unstable, which led to internal conflicts, rebellions, and fragmentation of the empire.

2. Foreign invasions :

  • The empire was weakened by an invasion of the Bactrian Greeks, as well as attacks by other foreign powers such as the Sakas, the Kushanas, and the Indo-Greeks.

3. Continuous revolts :

  • The empire experienced continuous revolts in different parts. The military weakness of the Mauryas made them incapable of facing foreign aggressions and of making the governors of the provinces responsible for good administration.
  • Assassination of the last Mauryan ruler Brihadratha, the last Mauryan ruler, was assassinated by his commander Pushyamitra Sungha.

Other factors that contributed to the collapse of the Maurya Empire include:

1. The empire’s size

2. Provincial independence

3. Internal insurrection

question: Gandhara School of art was developed during the reign of.......... (UPSC Prelims 1986)
option_1: Ashoka
option_2: Kanishka
option_3: Chandragupta
option_4: Bindusara
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • The Gandhara School of Art developed during the reign of the Kushana emperor Kanishka in the first century AD. The school’s flourishing period was from the first century BCE to the fourth century CE.
  • The Gandhara School is known for its first sculptural representations of the Buddha in human form. The school’s images are known for their anatomical accuracy, spatial depth, and foreshortening. The Buddha’s head in Gandhara art is very similar to the Greek God Apollo.
  • The Gandhara School incorporated many motifs and techniques from Classical Roman art in its interpretation of Buddhist legends. The school used black stone and stucco extensively. Some prominent free standing structures include Chaityas, Viharas, and Stupas.
  • The main centers where art pieces of Gandhara School have been found are: Jalalabad, Hadda, Bamaran, Begram, Taxila.

question: The ancient Indian coins were introduced during the.......... (UPSC Prelims 1986)
option_1: 3rd century AD
option_2: 3rd century BC
option_3: Kanishka’s period
option_4: Ashokas’s reign
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • The Coinage of India began anywhere between early 1st millennium BCE to the 6th century BCE, and consisted mainly of copper and silver coins in its initial stage. The coins of this period were Karshapanas or Pana.
  • A variety of earliest Indian coins, however, unlike those circulated in West Asia, were stamped bars of metal, suggesting that the innovation of stamped currency was added to a pre-existing form of token currency which had already been present in the Janapadas and Mahajanapada kingdoms of the Early historic India.
  • The kingdoms that minted their own coins included Gandhara, Kuntala, Kuru, Magadha, Panchala, Shakya, Surasena, Surashtra and Vidarbha etc.
  • The tradition of Indian coinage in the 2nd millennium evolved with Indo Islamic rule in India and the British Raj in the 19th century.

question:
Arrange the following in proper sequence.......... (UPSC Prelims 1986)

1. Vinayapitaka

2. Gita Govinda

3. Raghuvamsha

3. Atharvaved

The correct sequence is :
option_1: 3 2 1 4
option_2: 4 3 2 1
option_3: 4 1 3 2
option_4: 2 4 3 1
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

The Atharvaveda :

  • It is a sacred text and one of the four Vedas in Hinduism. It is the fourth Veda and is considered the “knowledge storehouse of atharvas”. The Atharvaveda is a collection of 20 books that include chants, spells, prayers, and hymns. It covers topics such as illnesses, rituals to remove anxieties and maladies, and prolonging life.
  • The Atharvaveda is the oldest literary monument of Indian medicine and is believed to be the origin of Ayurveda. It contains a variety of medicine stages of evolution, from the most primitive to some of the most highly developed.

The Vinaya Pitaka :

  • It is the first of three parts of the Tipitaka, the formal name for the earliest Buddhist teachings. The Vinaya Pitaka translates to “basket of discipline”.
  •  The Vinaya Pitaka is made up of five books: Pârâjika Pâli, Pâcittiya Pâli, Mahâvagga Pâli, Culavagga Pâli, Parivâra Pâli.

The Vinaya Pitaka is a Buddhist scripture that contains the following:

1. Rules of discipline for monks and nuns

2. Stories about the first Buddhist monks and nuns

3. An exposition of monastic rules and disciplinary actions

4. How to behave with each other and within society

  • The Vinaya Pitaka is one of three parts of the Tipitaka, along with the Sutta Pitaka and the Abhidhamma Pitaka.

Raghuvamsha :

  • It is a Sanskrit epic poem written by Kalidasa around the 5th century CE. The title translates to “Dynasty of Raghu”. The poem is one of two great court epics (mahakavyas) written by Kalidasa. It is made up of 19 cantos and about 1,570 verses.
  • The poem tells the stories of the Raghu dynasty, including the family of Dilipa and his descendants. The dynasty is named after Raghu, a legendary king who protected a sacrificial horse from Indra. The Raghuvanshi kings include Mandhata, Harishchandra, Sagara, Bhagiratha, Dilīpa, Raghu, Aja, Dasharatha, and Rama.

Gita Govinda :

  • It is a Sanskrit poem written by the 12th century Hindu poet Jayadeva. It describes the relationship between Krishna, Radha, and the gopis (female cow herders) of Vrindavan. The poem is known for its high literary value and religious longing, and is particularly popular with followers of Lord Vishnu.

question:
Arrange the following Buddhist councils in a chronological order.......... (UPSC Prelims 1986)

  1. Vaishali
  2. Rajagriha
  3. Patliputra
  4.  Sreenarar

The correct sequence is :
option_1: 1, 4, 3, 2
option_2: 3, 2, 1, 4
option_3: 4, 1, 2, 3
option_4: 2, 1, 3, 4
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

1. First Buddhist Council :

  • Held in Rajagriha in Magadha (modern Rajgir, Bihar state, India) in 400 BC. The council’s goal was to recite and compile the teachings of the Buddha and the monastic discipline.

2. Second Buddhist Council :

  • Held in Vaishali, India in 383 BC. The council was under the patronage of King Kalasoka and presided over by Sabakami. The council settled disputes on the Vinaya Pitaka and code of discipline.

3. Third Buddhist Council :

  • Held in Pataliputra, India around 250 BC. The council was under the patronage of King Asoka and presided over by Moggaliputta Tissa.

4. The Fourth Buddhist Council :

  • It was held in 72 AD at Kundalvana, Kashmir. The council was held under the patronage of Kushan king Kanishka. The council’s president was Vasumitra, and Aśvaghosa was his deputy.

 The council divided Buddhism into two sects:

1. Mahayana and

2. Hinayana.

  • All deliberations were conducted in Sanskrit, and Abhidhamma texts were translated from Prakrit to Sanskrit. The Buddhist councils began after the death of Siddhartha Gautama Buddha and have continued into the modern age.
  • The purpose of these councils is to resolve disciplinary and theological conflicts and to clarify the contents of sutras.

question: Metals known to Indus Valley Civilisation were.......... (UPSC Prelims 1987)
option_1: Gold, copper, silver but not iron
option_2: Silver, iron, bronze but not gold
option_3: Iron, copper, gold but not silver
option_4: Zinc, silver, iron but not gold
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The Indus Valley Civilization, also known as the Harappan Civilization, was a Bronze Age civilization. The metals known to the Indus Valley Civilization were: Gold, Copper, Silver, Bronze.
  • The Indus Valley Civilization existed from around 3300 BCE to 1300 BCE, which was before the Iron Age. The Indus Valley people or Harappan people did not discover iron. Iron was discovered in the post Vedic period.
  • The Harappan civilization used copper and stones to make weapons. The people of the Indus Valley also had spears with thin leaf shaped spearheads, short swords, daggers, and axes, all made of copper.

question:
Match the columns : (UPSC Prelims 1987)

Treaty of alliance between Bentinck and Ranjit Singh

Sites of Chalukyan architecture

  • The Treaty of Roaper of 1809 was an agreement between the British East India Company and Ranjit Singh, the Sikh leader who founded the Sikh empire.
  • Kumrahar is the name of an area of Patna, where remains of the ancient city of Pataliputra were excavated. It is located 5 km east of Patna Railway Station. Archaeological remains of the Mauryan period have been found.
  • Aihole is a historic site of Chalukyan architecture. The Chalukyas sponsored artisans and built many temples in this region between the 6th and 8th centuries.

question: Man Passed from the food gathering stage to the food producing stage in the......... (UPSC Prelims 1988)
option_1: Neolithic Age
option_2: Mesolithic Age
option_3: Chalcolithic Age
option_4: Palaeolithic Age
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Man transitioned from the food gathering stage to the food producing stage during the Neolithic Age, which began around 12,000 years ago. This transition led to the development of agriculture and the domestication of animals.
  • The Neolithic Age is the youngest period of Stone Age history and is characterized by the discovery of fire, the domestication of animals, and the storage of grains. The end of the last Ice Age also made the climate warmer and wetter, which provided better conditions for crops to grow.
  • The Neolithic Revolution made food production more efficient, but it also narrowed the diversity of foods available and decreased the quality of human nutrition. However, the increased efficiency allowed humans to invest their efforts in other activities.

The Paleolithic Age :

  • It is also known as the Old Stone Age, is a period in human prehistory that lasted from around 3.3 million years ago to 12,000 years ago. During this time, early humans developed basic stone tools and artifacts.

The Mesolithic Age :

  • It is also known as the Middle Stone Age, was a transitional period in human history that lasted from 10,000–5,000 BC. It was atime when human society changed from hunter-gatherer to agrarian/herder.

The Chalcolithic Age :

  • It is also known as the Copper Age, was a transitional period between the Neolithic and the Bronze Age. It lasted from around 3000 BCE to 500 BCE. The name comes from the words “chalco” (copper) and “lithic” (stone).
  • During the Chalcolithic Age, people began using metal and stone weapons, with copper being the first metal used. The Chalcolithic civilization is known for its predominantly red and orange wheel-made pottery.

Some Chalcolithic cultures include:

Ahar culture :

  • Also known as the Banas culture, this is among the earliest Chalcolithic cultures of India. The Ahar culture had a rich ceramic tradition consisting of Tan ware, thin Red ware, Black and Red ware, and Grey ware.

Malwa culture :

  • This was the main Chalcolithic culture of India, existing between 1900-1400 BC. The dominance of agriculture was an important feature of this culture.

Kayatha culture :

  • This culture represents the earliest known agriculture settlement in the present-day Malwa region. It also featured advanced copper metallurgy and stone blade industry.

question: Near the banks of which one of the following rivers the excavation in recent years has brought to light that Indus Valley Civilization percolated to far south? (UPSC Prelims 1988)
