question: The oldest Hindu epic is.......... (UPSC Prelims 1988)
option_1: Mahabhashya
option_2: Ramayana
option_3: Ashtadhyayi
option_4: Mahabharata
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • The Ramayana is the oldest Hindu epic. The Ramayana is about the life of Rama, who is an incarnation of the Hindu god Vishnu. Some believe the Ramayana was written 2,500 years ago, while others believe it was written around 1,800 years ago.
  • The Ramayana and the Mahabharata are some of the oldest surviving epic poems ever written. Both epics were originally written on palm leaves, but the authors, Valmiki and Veda Vyasa, also narrated the stories to their disciples. The Mahabharata was written down on paper first.
  • The Mahabharata is about issues of succession and the lead up to the Kurukshetra war, and then the war itself and the aftermath. The Mahabharata is believed to be a tale of the Indo-Iranian speaking Aryans who supposedly invaded northwest India/Pakistan at that time.

Mahabhashya :

  • It is a commentary on Sanskrit grammar rules from the 2nd century BCE. The title translates to “Great Commentary”. It is attributed to Patañjali and was written during the Shunga dynasty.
  • Mahabhashya is a commentary on selected rules from Pānini’s treatise, the Ashtadhyayi, and Kātyāyana’s Vārttika-sūtra. The Ashtadhyayi is a foundational text in the field of Sanskrit, and the Vārttika-sūtra is an elaboration of Pānini’s grammar.
  • Mahabhashya is both a defense of Panini against his chief critic and detractor Katyayana and a refutation of some of Panini’s aphorisms.

Ashtadhyayi :

  • It is a Sanskrit treatise on grammar written by the Indian grammarian Panini between the 6th and 5th century BCE. The title translates to “Eight Chapters”.
  • The text is a list of rules, including lists of verbs and suffixes. The lists have different headings that describe the behavior of the items they contain. The text is modeled on the dialect and register of elite speakers of the time, and also accounts for some features of the older Vedic language.

question: Who among the following was not sent to Lanka for the propagation of Buddhism? (UPSC Prelims 1988)
option_1: Rashtriya
option_2: Bhadrasaro
option_3: Uttriya
option_4: Sona
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • Sona was not sent to Sri Lanka for the propagation of Buddhism.
  • Ashoka, the Mauryan emperor of ancient India, sent his daughter Sanghamitra to Sri Lanka in the 3rd century BCE to spread Buddhism. Sanghamitra arrived in Sri Lanka with a branch of the Bodhi tree, and planted it in Anuradhapura, the ancient capital of Sri Lanka. The branch became a significant Buddhist site known as the Sri Maha Bodhi.
  • Ashoka also sent his son Mahendra to Sri Lanka to spread Buddhism. Ashoka sent many missionaries to spread Buddhism, including to Greece and Thailand.

question: Sculptures of the Mathura School of Art which flourishrd in the early centuries of the Christian Era are made out of.......... (UPSC Prelims 1988)
option_1: Marble
option_2: Slate stone
option_3: Granite
option_4: Red sandstone
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • The Mathura School of Art, which flourished in the early centuries of the Christian Era, produced sculptures made from red sandstone. The sculptures were often made from spotted red sandstone.
  • The Mathura School of Art produced many images, including Buddhas and bodhisattvas, and narrative reliefs. The school’s favored materials were gray slate or blue schist, and later phases also used stucco.
  • The Mathura School of Art flourished during the reign of Kushana ruler Kanishka in the first century AD. Mathura was the traditional center of output for the school, with Sarnath and Kosambi also playing key roles.
  • The Gandhara and Mathura schools of art are both Indian art schools that flourished during the Kushana Empire. The Gandhara School of Art was influenced by Greco Roman norms and Buddhism, while the Mathura School of Art was influenced by Hinduism, Buddhism, and Jainism.

Here are some other differences between the Gandhara and Mathura schools of art:

Materials :

  • The Gandhara School of Art used bluish gray and gray sandstone, stucco (lime plaster), and gray bluesists and green phyllite. The Mathura School of Art used spotted red sandstone and red stone for making sculptures.

Themes :

  • The Gandhara School of Art predominantly depicted Buddhist themes, including images of the Buddha, bodhisattvas, and Buddhist narrative scenes. The Mathura School of Art focused on Hindu and Buddhist themes, including gods, goddesses, and religious narratives.

Influences :

  • The Gandhara School of Art was influenced by Greek methodologies and possibly Macedonian. The Mathura School of Art was influenced by Indianism and the early Indian Buddhist arts of Bharhut and Sanchi of MP.

question: The earliest evidence of a Jewish community setting in India is provided by a charter of a.......... (UPSC Prelims 1988)
option_1: Chola king
option_2: Pandya kig
option_3: Chera king
option_4: Viayanagara king
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • The earliest evidence of a Jewish community settling in India is provided by a charter of a Vijayanagara king.
  • The Jewish copper plates of Cochin, also known as the Cochin plates of Bhaskara Ravi-varman, is a royal charter issued by the Chera Perumal king of Kerala to Joseph Rabban, a Jewish merchant magnate of Kodungallur.
  • The oldest surviving documents of the Cochin Jewish community date to 1000 CE, and it’s safe to presume the community was present well before then.
  • The three main communities of Jews in India are the Cochinis, the Bene Israel, and the Baghdadis.

question: The Lingayat movement owes its origin to.......... (UPSC Prelims 1988)
option_1: Vidyaranya
option_2: Purandharadasa
option_3: Appar
option_4: Basava
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • The Lingayat movement, which arose in the 12th century, is attributed to Basava and his nephew Channabasava. Basava was a Brahmana minister in the court of a Kalachuri ruler, and his followers were known as Virashaivas or Lingayats. The movement was inspired by the Nayanars and emphasized personal religious experience over text-based dogmatism.
  • Basava revolted against the Hindu philosophy and its caste system, which he believed was the cause of social and economic slavery of the masses. He distinguished between religion and communalism in the light of monotheism.
  • The Lingayat movement gave access to divinity to all men and women, regardless of their caste, creed, or color.

question:
Match the columns: (UPSC Prelims 1988)

List - I

 

List - II

A.  Amaravati

1. 

 Hoyasalas

B.  Aihole

2. 

 Cholas

C.  Thanjavur

3. 

 Satavahanas

D.  Halebid

4. 

 Chalukyas

 

5. 

 Pandyas

Select the correct answer from the codes given below :

A B C D
option_1: 2 4 5 1
option_2: 3 1 4 2
option_3: 3 4 2 1
option_4: 4 5 2 3
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

The Correct option is (C) 3 4 2 1.

The Hoysalas :

  • They were a Kannadiga power that ruled most of present-day Karnataka from the 10th to 14th centuries. The Hoysalas originated in the Western Ghats, and emerged as borderland chiefs during the conflict between the Western Chalukya Empire and the Cholas.
  • The Hoysalas sided with the Chalukyas and were made provincial governors. The Hoysalas built many Hindu and Jain temples during their nearly 200-year rule over South India.
  • The Hoysala dynasty was founded by Sala, and the greatest ruler was Vishnuvardhana (Bittideva).
  • Vishnuvardhana defeated the Cholas in the battle of Talakadu, and built the kirtinarayana temple to commemorate his victory.
  • The Hoysala Empire was succeeded by the Vijayanagar dynasty.

The Cholas :

  • They were a Tamil dynasty that ruled the Chola Empire from the 9th to 13th century. The Cholas were based in the Kaveri River delta and were one of the three ruling families in the region during the first two centuries CE.
  • The Cholas were one of the oldest and longest ruling dynasties in the world. They were a powerful kingdom that extended their control to Sri Lanka and the Malay peninsula. The Cholas played an active part in the Hindu cultural influence seen today in southeast Asia.
  • The Cholas invested in art, literature, education, and architecture. They built massive stone temples that served as cultural and social hubs. They also designed man-made lakes to alleviate droughts and provide a supply of safe drinking water.
  • The last ruler of the Chola Dynasty was Rajendra Chola III, who ruled from 1246 to 1279 AD.

The Satavahanas :

  • They were an ancient Indian dynasty that ruled from the mid-1st century BCE to the early 3rd century CE. The dynasty’s capital was Pratishthana (modern Paithan) in the Aurangabad district of Maharashtra.
  • The Satavahanas ruled over present day Andhra Pradesh, Maharashtra, and Telangana. They also ruled over parts of Gujarat, Karnataka, and Madhya Pradesh.
  • The Satavahanas were the first native Indian kings to issue their own coins. The coins had the rulers’ portraits on them and the coin legends were in Prakrit. Some reverse coin legends are in Tamil, Telugu, and Kannada.
  • The Satavahanas’ official language was Prakrit, but the script was Brahmi. The Satavahanas also used Sanskrit in political inscriptions, but rarely.
  • The Satavahanas followed the Vedic traditions, but they believed in harmonyand encouraged other religions like Buddhism and Jainism. The Satavahanas promoted art literature, and education literature was created in Prakrit language.

The Chalukya dynasty :

  • It was a Hindu dynasty that ruled parts of southern and central India from the 6th to the 12th century.
  • During this period, they ruled as three related yet individual dynasties:

Badami Chalukyas :

  • The earliest dynasty, with their capital at Badami (Vatapi) in Karnataka. They ruled from the mid-6th century and declined after the death of their greatest king, Pulakesin II in 642 AD.

Western Chalukyas :

  • Ruled as emperors in the Deccan (i.e., peninsular India) from 543 to 757 CE and again from about 975 to about 1189.

Eastern Chalukyas :

  • Emerged after the death of Pulakesin II in Eastern Deccan with capital at Vengi. They ruled in Vengi (in eastern Andhra Pradesh state) from about 624 to about 1070.

The Pandyas : 

  • They were a Tamil dynasty that ruled the southernmost part of India from before the 3rd century BCE until the 17th century CE. They were one of four great kingdoms of Tamilakam, along with the Pallavas, the Cholas, and the Cheras.
  • The Pandyas were known for their contributions to Indian literature and sculpture. They also excelled in maritime trade, with their reach extending from Rome and Greece to China, Malaysia, and the Maldives. The Pandyas produced some of the finest pearls along the South Indian coast.
  • The Pandyas were initially Jains, but later became Hindus, specifically Shaivas. Their main city was Madurai, where Tamil kings held three Sangam poetry academies.

question:
Match the columns: (UPSC Prelims 1988)

List- I 

List- II

A. Rig  Veda

1.  Musical hymns

B. Yajur Veda

2.  Hymns and rituals

C. Sama Veda

3.  Charms and  spells

D. Atharva Veda

4.  Hymns and  prayers

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Select the correct answer from the codes given below :

A B C D
option_1: 4 2 1 3
option_2: 3 2 1 4
option_3: 4 1 2 3
option_4: 2 3 1 4
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Rigveda is the oldest of all four Vedas. Rigveda is a collection of hymns and prayers. Rigveda contains 10 mandals and 1028 hymns.
  • Samveda is a collection of musical hymns. The earliest evidence of singing is found here.
  • Yajurved is called a book of prayers as it contains hymns and rituals.
  • Atharvaveda is called the book of magic. It contains charms and spells to ward off evils and diseases.

question:
Assertion (A) : In the Ancient period of Indian history urbanisation was followed by pastoral life 

Reason (R) : The Harappan Culture was an urban one (UPSC Prelims 1988)
option_1: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
option_2: Both A and R are true but R is not a correct explanation of A
option_3: A is true but R is false
option_4: A is false but R is true
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • Urbanization refers to the process of the growth and development of cities. The ancient period of Indian history witnessed the rise of several urban centers, particularly during the Indus Valley Civilization or the Harappan culture.
  • These cities, such as Harappa and Mohenjo Daro, were well-planned and had advanced features like drainage systems, public baths, and granaries. On the other hand, pastoral life refers to a way of life that revolves around raising livestock and herding animals.
  • It is a more rural and nomadic lifestyle compared to settled urban living. In the context of ancient Indian history, it is generally believed that urbanization during the Indus Valley Civilization was followed by a decline and a shift towards a more pastoral way of life.
  • This is supported by archaeological evidence such as the abandonment of urban centers and the emergence of smaller rural settlements in the later periods. The Harappan culture, also known as the Indus Valley Civilization, was one of the earliest urban civilizations in the Indian subcontinent. It flourished around 2600 1900 BCE in the region that is now Pakistan and northwest India.

Key features of the Harappan culture include:

1. Urban Centers: The civilization had well-planned urban centers with grid like street patterns and advanced in frastructure.

2. Advanced Architecture: The cit ies had well-designed houses, public buildings, and even multi-story struc tures.

3. Trade and Commerce: The presence of standardized weights, seals, and the discovery of artifacts from distant re gions suggest a well-developed trade network.

4. Writing System: The Harappans had a script that is yet to be deciphered fully.

5. Drainage Systems: The cities had sophisticated underground drainage systems.

question: The original name of mahabharata was.......... (UPSC Prelims 1989)
option_1: Brihit Katha
option_2: Sahastra Samhita
option_3: Jaya Samhita
option_4: Rajatarangini
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The original name of the Mahabharata was Jaya Samhita. The word “Jaya” means victory, and “Samhita” means collection. The Mahabharata is a Sanskrit epic that tells the story of the struggle between the Kaurava and the Pandava princes and their successors.
  • The Mahabharata was originally written by Lord Ganesha and narrated by Maharishi Ved Vyasa. It originally had 8,800 verses, but today it has 100,000. Some say that the Mahabharata of today is a corrupted version of Jaya Samhita.
  • The Mahabharata is a Sanskrit epic written by Vyasa and is also known as the Shaatasahasri-Samhita and Jaisamhita. The Mahabharata is considered one of the two major epics, the other being Ramayana. The Mahabharata is a valuable asset of Hindu culture and is also known as Shatsahastri Sanhita. The Mahabharata has one lakh verses and shlokas.

Brikhatkathā (Sanskrit for “the Great Narrative”) :

  • It is a collection of tales written by Gunadhya in the 2nd century. The original text was in Prakrit, but only Sanskrit versions remain today. The epic is said to have deviated from the popular themes of kings, queens, and their courts. Brihatkatha is said to have inspired many modern tales and legends, including Somadeva’s Kathā-sarit-sāgara (“Ocean of Rivers of Stories”).
  • Brihatkatha-manjari is a prose work by Kshemendra that is an abridgement of the northwestern recension of the lost Brihatkatha. It tells the story of the Gupta king Vikramaditya (Chandragupta II) around 400 AD.

Rajatarangini :

  • It is a Sanskrit epic poem and historical chronicle of early India. The title translates to “River of Kings” or “Waterway of Kings”. It was written by Kashmiri historian Kalhana in the 12th century. The book covers the entire history of the Kashmir region from its earliest times to the date of its composition.
  • Rajatarangini is divided into eight books called Tarangas and comprises 7826 verses. The opening verses of each book begin with prayers to the Hindu god Shiva.
  • Rajatarangini gives Administration, Social, Political and Economic information about the kings of Kashmir.

question: Which of the following is not a feature of Gandhara School of art ? (UPSC Prelims 1989)
option_1: It was patronised by Sakas and Kushanas
option_2: It has Indian and Greek influence
option_3: It resulted in the origination of Hinayanism
option_4: It portrayed human figures exactly
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

1. In this school of art the life size statues of Gautama Buddha were carved. Until then, the Buddhist existence was shown in the form of symbols like lotus, umbrella etc.

2. While carving the statues utmost care was given to the symmetry of the body including the muscles and moustaches which were shown in a natural setting.

3. In the specimens of the craftsmanship of this art-the folds and turns of the clothes were exhibited with minute care and skill.

4. In this art the ornaments that were carved on the statues received much attention which added to the physical beauty of the statue.

5. Polishing the statues is an important feature of this art.

6. The specimens were mostly prepared in stone, terracotta and clay.

7. The technique used in making the statues was Greek but, the idea, inspiration and personality all were Indian.

question: Jainism is differed from Buddhism in which of the following aspects?  (UPSC Prelims 1989)
option_1: Faith in Karma
option_2: Doctrine of Ahimsa
option_3: Belief in Fasting
option_4: Rejection of yagna
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

Jainism and Buddhism differ in several aspects, including:

1. Belief in karma :

  • Jains believe in karma, the law of cause and effect, while Buddhists do not. In Jainism, karma is a real substance that enters the body with each action.

2. Belief in the soul :

  • Jains believe in the existence of an eternal soul, while Buddhism denies the concept of self or soul.

3. Belief in reincarnation :

  • Buddhism does not believe in the transmigration of the soul, or reincarnation.

4. Approach to life :

  • Buddhism prescribes a middle path, while Jainism advocates a life of complete austerity.

5. Fasting :

  • Jainism lays great stress on fasting and self-torture, while Buddhism preaches the middle path. Jains believe in moderation when it comes to eating, and perform austerities as a way to eliminate karma.

6. Doctrine of Ahimsa :

  • Late medieval Jain scholars re-examined the doctrine of Ahimsa when faced with external threat or violence.

question: “Mitakshara” Is associated with.......... (UPSC Prelims 1989)
option_1: Yajnawalkya
option_2: Poetics
option_3: Drama
option_4: Grammar
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The Mitakshara is a commentary on the Yajnavalkya Smriti, an ancient Hindu text on law. It was written by Vijñāneśvara in the 12th century and is known for its theory of “inheritance by birth”. The Mitakshara is considered a primary authority on Hindu law, along with the Dāyabhāga.
  • The Mitakshara is a treatise on ancient Hindu law of inheritance, and discusses the birthright of sons to their ancestral property. It also includes the right of joint ownership of property, the right to joint possession and enjoyment of the property, the right to claim partition, the right to seek maintenance, and the right to challenge improper alienation made by Karta.

question: The Prakrit text “Gathasaptasati”is attributed to the Satvahana King.......... (UPSC Prelims 1989)
option_1: Vasishtiputra Pulumavi
option_2: Hala
option_3: Gautamiputra Satkarni
option_4: Amaru
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • The Prakrit text “Gathasaptasati” is attributed to the Satavahana king Hala.
  • Hala was a ruler of the Satavahana dynasty, which was a prominent dynasty in ancient South India from the 1st century BCE to the 3rd century CE.
  • The Gathasaptasati is an ancient collection of Indian poems in Maharashtri Prakrit language. It consists of 700 verses, all written in Prakrit. The name Gathasaptashati means 700 verses written in the gatha form.
  • The Gathasaptasati is a volume of poetry, mostly written by women, and was supposedly collected and edited by theSatvahana king, Hala. The poems are about love and are written as frank monologues usually by a married woman, or an unmarried girl.
  • The Gathasaptasati is literary work that is of utmost importance for the social and cultural history of Satavahanas.

question:
Match The Following: (UPSC Prelims 1989)

A. Chaitya 

1. Amaravati

B. Stupa    

2.  Besnagar

C. Pillar     

3.  Karle

D. Brick Temple

4. Bhitragaon

 

 

 

 

 

Select the correct answer from the codes given below :

A B C D
option_1: 1 3 4 2
option_2: 2 3 4 1
option_3: 3 1 2 4
option_4: 3 1 4 2
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

A chaitya :

  • It is a prayer hall, shrine, or sanctuary in Indian religions. The term is most commonly used in Buddhism.
  • In Buddhism, a chaitya is a space with a stupa, a rounded apse, and a high, rounded roof. The word “chaitya” comes from the Sanskrit word chita, which means “a pyre” or “a pile of ashes”.
  • Chaityas are often rectangular halls with polished walls, pillars, and a semi-circular roof. They have a doorway in the center, a window on each side, and a broad sill or ledge with a sun window above.
  • Chaityas are believed to have developed from the second century BC to the 10th century AD. The Karla Caves, Ajanta Caves, Ellora Caves, Udayagiri and Khandagiri Caves, Aurangabad Caves, and the Pandavleni Caves are some of the most important chaitya halls.

A stupa :

  • It is a mound-like or hemispherical structure that is used as a place of meditation. The word “stupa” is Sanskrit for “heap”. Stupas are an important form of Buddhist architecture, though they predate Buddhism.
  • Stupas are considered sacred monuments and contain relics, such as the remains of Buddhist monks or nuns. They are also places of burial or receptacles for religious objects. The decorations on the surrounding railings and gateways depict events from Buddha’s life.

Some examples of stupas include:

1. The Great Stupa at Sanchi

2. The Dhamek Stupa at Sarnath

3. The Mahabodhi Temple

  • The Amaravati Stupa is a ruined Buddhist monument in the village of Amaravathi, Andhra Pradesh, India. It is also known as the great stupa at Amarāvathi.

The Besnagar Pillar Inscription:

  • It is also known as the Garudadhwaja, is a stone pillar constructed by Heliodorus in the 2nd century BCE. The pillar is located in Vidisha, a city in central Madhya Pradesh, India.
  • The pillar has two inscriptions in Brahmi script. One inscription states that Heliodorus, an ambassador of the Indo Greek king Antialcidas, erected the pillar in honor of Vasudeva (Krishna). The second inscription is from the Mahabharata and mentions the virtues of “restraint, renunciation and rectitude”.
  • The inscription helps to prove the historicity of Krishna.

Bhitargaon Temple :

  • It is a 1,500-year-old brick temple in Kanpur, Uttar Pradesh. It’s one of the earliest surviving brick temples in India, built in the 5th century during the Gupta Empire.
  • The temple is made almost entirely of brick and mud mortar, and features a terracotta panel, a high Sikhara, and detailed terracotta sculptures in the niches on the outer walls. The temple is almost seventy feet tall, with a tapering shikhara fifty feet tall.
  • The temple’s decorations are similar to those found on the Nirvana temple at Kasia, which could date back to the Gupta era. The temple’s janda is decorated with sunken rectangular niches containing terracotta figures.

question: “Mattavilasa” is written by.......... (UPSC Prelims 1989)
option_1: Harsha
option_2: Rajshekhara
option_3: Somadeva
option_4: Mahendravarman I
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • Mahendravarman I wrote Mattavilasa Prahasana in the early seventh century. The play is a satire that mocks the Kapalika and Pasupata Saivite sects, Buddhists, and Jainism. The play is set in Kanchipuram, the capital of the Pallava kingdom, and centers on the drunken antics of a Kapalika mendicant and his wife.
  • Mahendravarman I was a ruler of the Pallava Empire from 600–630 AD. He is also credited with writing another play, Bhagavadajjuka.

question:
Assertion (A) :  The Form of Goverment in Rig vedic period was monarchy

Reason (R) :  Priest enjoyed both social and political status and influenced administration (UPSC Prelims 1989)
option_1: Both A and R are true and R is the correct explanation of A
option_2: Both A and R are true but R is not correct explanation  of A
option_3: A is true but R is False
option_4: A is false but R is true
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • The polity of the Early Vedic period was basically a tribal polity with the tribal chief in the centre which was transformed towards Monarchy in later Vedic period. Tribal assemblies, eg the Gana, Vidhata, Sabha and Samiti are mentioned in the RV.
  • The Sabha may have been the council of select clan members while the Samiti was a general tribal assembly, comprising the whole clan.
  • These assemblies performed the functions of the government and were also involved in the selection of the raja from the clansmen. So (A) is not completely true. (R) is definitely true.

question:
Match the following :  (UPSC Prelims 1989)                              

 A. Charak

1. Samkhya

 B. Nagarjuna

2. Sanskrit author

 C. Kapila

3. Ayuerveda

 D.Gorakhnath

4. Rasaratnakara

Select the correct answer from the codes given below :

A B C D
option_1: 1 2 3 4
option_2: 2 1 3 4
option_3: 4 2 1 3
option_4: 3 4 1 2
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

Maharishi Charaka :

  • He was a physician who lived between 100 BC and AD 150-200. He was a court physician to Kanishka the Great and a key contributor to Ayurveda, a system of medicine and lifestyle developed in Ancient India.
  • Charaka is known for editing the medical treatise Charaka Samhita, which is one of the basic books of traditional Indian medicine and Ayurveda. The text has eight books, totaling 120 chapters. It deals with subjects such as fetal generation and development, anatomy of the human body, and function and malfunction of thebody according to the tridosha (the three humors of the body)—vata, pitta, and kapha. Charaka is sometimes referred to as the Father of Indian Medicine.

Nagarjuna :

  • He was an Indian metallurgist and alchemist who wrote Rasaratnakara in the eighth century A.D.. The book is a treatise about the preparation of mercury compounds and the status of alchemy and metallurgy in the region. It also describes methods for extracting metals like gold, silver, tin, and copper.

Kapila :

  • He is also known as Maharishi Kapila or Cakradhanus, is a Vedic sage and religious teacher in Hindu tradition. He is said to have lived in the Indian subcontinent around the 6th or 7th century BCE.
  • Kapila is often considered one of the founders of the Samkhya system, one of six systems of Indian philosophy. He is also mentioned in the Ramayana, the holy book of Hinduism.
  • Kapila is said to have been the son of Sage Kardama and Devahuti, the daughter of Svayambhuva Manu, the ruler of the earth.

Gorakhnath (c. early 11th century) :

  • He is a Hindu yogi and saint who is known for founding the Nath Hindu monastic movement in India. He is also known by the Sanskrit form of his name,
  • Gorakhanātha. Gorakhnath is considered one of the two disciples of Matsyendranath. He is said to have lived sometime between 900 and 1225 CE. He is the main propagator and organizer of the order of Yogis that mainly practice Hatha Yoga. He is also the traditional author of the first treatise on Hatha Yoga, which is now lost.
  • The Nath tradition emphasizes physical practices, breath control, meditation, and the development of spiritual knowledge and awareness.

question: The Harappans did not know the use of.......... (UPSC Prelims 1990)
option_1: Bronze
option_2: Gold
option_3: Iron
option_4: Silver
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The Harappans, also known as the Indus Valley people, did not know the use of iron. The Harappans were aware of most metals, including gold, silver, copper, and bronze. However, they did not discover iron.
  • The Harappans were a bronze age civilization that made many advances in copper and bronze.
  • They manufactured gold and silver objects, but silver was more common than gold. The Indus Valley Civilization existed from 2500 BC to 1750 BC.

question: Who among the following was not a writter of legal texts? (UPSC Prelims 1990)
option_1: Yajnavalkya
option_2: Manu
option_3: Narada
option_4: Bharavi
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • Narada was not a writer of legal texts. Narada is a Vedic sage who is known for traveling and sharing news and wisdom.

question: In which one of the follwing inscription is Ashoka mentioned by the name? (UPSC Prelims 1990)
option_1: Major Rock edict at Girnar
option_2: Minor Rock inscription at Maski
option_3: Bairat inscription
option_4: Lamghan inscription
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • Ashoka’s name is mentioned in the Minor Rock inscription at Maski. The inscription is located on a large granite boulder in Raichur, Karnataka, India. It was discovered in 1915 by C. Beadon and later studied by archaeologist Robert Sewell.
  • The inscription is written in the Brahmi script. The inscription confirms the association of the title “Devanampriya” with “Asoka”.
  • The first line of the inscription reads “Devanampriyam Asoka”, which translates to “Beloved of the God” and “name of Ashoka”.
  • Other inscriptions refer to Ashoka as “Devanampriyam” (beloved of God) and “Priyadarshi” (loved by all).

The Major Rock Edicts of Ashoka :

  • They are a collection of 14 edicts, plus two additional edicts found in Odisha. The edicts are written in Prakrit and Greek, and are located on the frontiers of Ashoka’s territory, rather than in the Mauryan heartland.

Some of the major rock edicts include:

  •  Rock Edict I: Prohibits animal sacrifices, especially during festivals
  •  Rock Edict II: Discusses medical treatment for humans and animals, planting medicinal herbs and fruits, and digging wells
  •  Rock Edict III: Discusses generosity to Brahmins
  •  Rock Edict VI: Discusses the welfare and happiness of the people

The Minor Rock Edicts of Ashoka include:

  • Bairat, Bahapur, Shasram/Sasaram, Ahraura, Rupnath, Panguraria/ Pangudariya, Gujjara, Maski, Gavimath, Brahmagiri.
  • The edicts were created to communicate Ashoka’s governance principles and policies to his subjects. They are a valuable historical source that provides insights into the governance and moral philosophy of one of ancient India’s most renowned rulers. Many of the edicts emphasize the principles of non-violence, compassion, and moral conduct associated with Buddhism.

Bairat inscription :

  • Its including the Buddhist Monastery remains at Beejak ki pahari and the Bairath Ashoka Rock Edict.
  • The Buddhist Monastery remains at Beejak ki pahari is a historical landmark that is famous for meditation.

The Aramaic Inscription of Laghman :

  • It is an inscription on a rock in the Laghman Valley of Afghanistan. It was written in Aramaic by the Indian emperor Ashoka around 260 BCE. The inscription is also known as the Pul-i-Darunteh Aramaic inscription and the Lampaka inscription.
  • The Aramaic Inscription of Laghman is the oldest known Ashoka inscription, dating back to the 10th year of Ashoka’s reign. Another Aramaic inscription, the Laghman II inscription, was discovered nearby in 1974. The inscription slab is now housed in the Kabul museum.

question: The First Buddhist Council met at.......... (UPSC Prelims 1990)
option_1: Rajagriha
option_2: Pataliputra
option_3: Jalandhara
option_4: Kashmir
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The first Buddhist Council is said to have taken place at Rājagriha (modern Rājgīr, Bihār state, India) during the first rainy season after the Buddha’s death. The council was held in 483 BC at the Sattapani caves (Sattaparnaguha) in Rajagriha.
  • The council was presided over by Mahakassapa, one of the Buddha’s most senior disciples. The council’s main objective was to preserve the Buddha’s teachings.
  • The council’s purpose was to recite and agree on the Buddha’s actual teachings and on proper monastic discipline. The council lasted for 7 months.

question: Which one of the following can be classified as a “Pratiloma Marriage”? (UPSC Prelims 1990)
option_1: A Brahmin girl marrying a Sudra boy
option_2: A Sudra girl marrying a Brahmin boy
option_3: A Brahmin girl marrying a Brahmin boy
option_4: None of these
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Pratiloma is a Sanskrit term that describes a marriage between a man of a lower social class and a woman of a higher one. The term is used in the Manusmriti, also known as the Laws of Manu. The term also refers to any offspring of the marriage.
  • The name comes from the idea that the marriage is out of line with the natural order, unlike an anuloma union.
  • In Hindu culture, hypogamy, or Pratiloma, is not traditionally accepted and is even discouraged. However, it is a growing trend due to the advancement of women and their status.
  • Shastrakara Manu believed that the offspring of the most hated Pratiloma would become “chandalas” or “untouchables”.

question: The Stone Age people had the first domestic.......... (UPSC Prelims 1990)
option_1: Asses
option_2: Dogs
option_3: Horses
option_4: Sheep
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • The dog is widely considered the first domesticated animal by Stone Age people, with the process beginning in the Mesolithic Period (Middle Stone Age) in Central Asia over 14,000 years ago, and later followed by sheep and goats in the Neolithic Period. 
  • Dogs were likely domesticated from wolves and were used to help protect humans from predators.
  • Timing: Domestication of dogs began in the Mesolithic Period, the middle part of the Stone Age. 
  • Origin: Evidence suggests dogs were first domesticated in Central Asia. 
  • Purpose: These early dogs helped protect humans from larger predators. 
  • Later domestication: After dogs, sheep and goats were domesticated later in the Neolithic Period (New Stone Age).

question:
Match List I with List II and select the correct answer using the codes given below them: (UPSC Prelims 1990)

List I (works) : List II (Author) 

List I

List II

A. Dayabhaga

1.  Harsha

B. Mitakshara

2.  Jimutavahana

C. Nagananda

3.  Krittivasa

D. Ramayana 

4. Vijnanesvara

Select the correct answer from the codes given below:

A B C D
option_1: 2 3 1 4
option_2: 2 4 1 3
option_3: 4 2 3 1
option_4: 4 1 2 3
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • Jimutavahana, a medieval Hindu jurist, wrote the Hindu law treatise Dāyabhāga. The treatise focuses on inheritance procedures.
  • The Dayabhaga School of Hindu Law is primarily followed in West Bengal and other parts of eastern India. It is based on Jimutavahana’s commentary on the Yajnavalkya Smriti.
  • Vijñāneśvara, a scholar in the Western Chalukya court, wrote the Mitāksarā in the late eleventh and early twelfth century.
  • The Mitāksarā is a legal commentary on the Yajnavalkya Smriti and is known for its theory of “inheritance by birth”. The Mitāksarā is a treatise on inheritance, the law of primogeniture, and family feuds.It is observed in all parts of India and is subdivided into the Benares, the Mithila, the Maharashtra, and the Dravida schools.
  • The Mitāksarā and the Dāyabhāga are both ancient Indian legal texts. The Dāyabhāga states that the father is the sole ruler of all property, both ancestral and personal.
  • Harshavardhana, also known as Harsha, wrote the Sanskrit play Nagananda (Joy of the Serpents). Harshavardhana was a member of the Pushyabhuti dynasty and ruled a large empire in northern India from 606 to 647 CE. He was a Buddhist convert and a lover of education and learning.
  • Nagananda is considered one of the most acclaimed Sanskrit dramas. Harshavardhana also wrote two other plays, Priyadarshika and Ratnavali.
  • The Krittivasi Ramayan is a Bengali adaptation of the original Valmiki Ramayana, written in Sanskrit. Krittibas Ojha (1381–1461) was a medieval Bengali poet who composed the Krittivasi Ramayan in the fifteenth century CE.
  • The Valmiki Ramayana is considered the primary version of the epic Ramayana, written by Sage Valmiki in the 5th century BCE. The Krittibas Ramayana is a well known version of the Ramayana in Bengal, with a basic story and characters that are the same as the Valmiki Ramayana.

question:
Consider  the following statement regarding Kautilya’s Arthshastra........... (UPSC Prelims 1990)

  1. It Places Morality higher than political expediency
  2. It argues the case for a fully centralised goverment for the empire
  3. It deals with problem of administration as well laws of property and crime
  4. It includes section on goverment of the state and diplomatic relation with other states

 Of these statement :
option_1: 1, 2 and 3 are correct
option_2: 2 and 3 are correct
option_3: 3 and 4 are correct
option_4: 1, 2 and 4 are correct
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • Kautilya’s Arthashastra is a Sanskrit treatise on statecraft, economic policy, and military strategy. It is also considered a book of political realism, analyzing how the political world works and what a king must do to preserve the state.
  • The book is traditionally attributed to Kautilya, also known as Vishnugupta and Chanakya. He was the prime minister of India’s first great emperor, Chandragupta Maurya.
  • The book is said to provide insight into the ancient Indian anthology of political wisdom, theory, and the art of statecraft. It is also said to prescribe building alliances and entering into treaties with other countries to increase power peacefully.
  • According to Kautilya, people should follow their dharma and practice the virtues of honesty and truthfulness.

question: Which one of the following was the contemporary Bengal king during the time of  Harsha of  Kannauj? (UPSC Prelims 1990)
option_1: Bhaskaravarman
option_2: Divakaramitra
option_3: Devagupta
option_4: Sasanka
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • Sasanka was the contemporary Bengal king during the time of Harsha of Kannauj. Sasanka was the leader of the Gauda Dynasty and the first sovereign ruler of Bengal.
  • He is a major figure in Bengali history and reigned in the 7th century AD. Some historians place his rule between 590 AD and 625 AD.
  • Sasanka was the principal sovereign leader of Bengal and killed Rajyavardhana, the elder brother of Harshavardhana.
  • Harsha swore vengeance on Sasanka and marched towards Kannauj with a largearmy. On the way, he met an emissary of Bhaskara Varman, king of Kamrupa, and entered into an alliance with that State. Harsha formed an alliance with Bhaskar Varman and forced Shashanka to retreat.

question: It is said that of the five Doctrines taught by jainism four were taken over by Mahavira from previous teachers and only one was added by him.Which one of the following was the doctrine added by him? (UPSC Prelims 1991)
option_1: Do not commit violence
option_2: Observe continence
option_3: Do not acquie property
option_4: Do not speak a lie
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • Mahavira, the 24th Tirthankara of Jainism, is said to have added the fifth principle of Jainism, Brahmacharya, to the four principles of his predecessors.

The four principles are:

1. Ahimsa: Do not hurt any living thing

2. Satya: Always tell the truth

3. Asteya: Do not steal

4. Aparigraha: Do not possess any wealth

  • Mahavira taught that observing these vows is necessary for spiritual liberation. These principles have influenced Jain culture in many ways, such as leading to a predominantly lacto-vegetarian lifestyle.

question: Which one of the following does not indicate the difference between Mahayanism and Hinayanism? (UPSC Prelims 1991)
option_1: Belief in the Boddhisattvas
option_2: Worship of the image of Buddha
option_3: Use of the Sanskrit language
option_4: Admission of  women into the Monasteries
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • Admission of women into monasteries is not a difference between Mahayana and Hinayana Buddhism. The founder of Buddhism, Gautama Buddha, allowed women to join his monastic community and participate fully.
  • Hinayana and Mahayana are two major sects of Buddhism that split from the religion in 72 AD.

The main difference between the two is their view of Gautama Buddha:

Mahayana :

  • Believes Buddha was a divine being who guided his followers to nirvana. Mahayana Buddhists also worship bodhisattvas and believe that Buddha was sent to earth so that people could learn from him.

Hinayana :

  • Believes Buddha was an ordinary human being who attained nirvana. Hinayana Buddhists believe that salvation is only for those strong souls who don’t need external aid or worship.
  • Hinayana is a term used by the early Mahayana movement to describe established Buddhist schools. Hinayana literally means “lesser vehicle”.

question: Indus Valley civilisation is also known as the Harappan culture because.......... (UPSC Prelims 1991)
option_1: The site of Harappa is a six times larger than Mohenjodaro site
option_2: The Indus valley civilisation is cosidered the elementary /initial stage of the vedic culture and Harappa is belived to be the same as Harappa mentioned in the vedas
option_3: Harappa was the first site to be excavated in the Indian valley
option_4: The Most Important /significant evidence of the achivements of this civilisation have been excavated from Harappa
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • The Indus Valley Civilization is also known as the Harappan culture because Harappawas the first site to be excavated and is believed to be the capital city.
  • The Indus Valley Civilization was discovered in 1920–22 with the excavation of two of its most important sites:

1) Harappa

2) Mohenjodaro.

  • The Indus Valley Civilization is the earliest known urban culture of the Indian subcontinent. The cities flourished near the Indus River. The Indus Valley civilization was famous for its drainage system, with each house having its own drainage that connected to the main drainage.

question: The Upanishads are collectively known as......... (UPSC Prelims 1991)
option_1: Vedangas
option_2: Vedanta
option_3: Sruti
option_4: Smriti
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • The Upanishads are also known as Vedanta. The word “Vedanta” literally means “the end of Veda”. The Upanishads are the concluding parts of the Vedas, and they came at the end of the Vedic period.
  • The Upanishads are philosophical texts that deal with ritual observance and the individual’s place in the universe. They are derived from the words upa (near) and shad (to sit).
  • Vedanta is one of the six systems of Indian philosophy. It encompasses the ideas that emerged from, or were aligned with, the speculations and enumerations contained in the Upanishads. Vedanta has metaphysical and mystical elements that teach about the nature of existence and illusion.
  • The Upanishads are also known as Shruti Literature, which is a type of Vedic literature. Shruti Literature is canonical and consists of revelation and unquestionable truth.

question: Which one of the following archaeologists initially discovered the Mohanjedaro site of the indus valley civilisation? (UPSC Prelims 1991)
option_1: Sujohu Marshall
option_2: Daya Ram Sahni
option_3: Rakhal Das Banerji
option_4: Sir Mortimer Wheeler
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • Rakhal Das Banerji discovered the Mohenjo-daro site in 1922. Banerji was an officer of the Archaeological Survey of India.
  • The Indus Valley Civilization flourished from 2500 BC to 1750 BC. The Mohenjo daro site is located in the Larkana district of Sindh in Pakistan.

question:
Match List I with List II and select the correct answer using the codes given below the Lists: (UPSC Prelims 1991)

List I

List II

A. Chandragupta          Maurya

1. The great council   of prayag

B. Samudragupta 

2. Jainism

C.Harsha Vardhan

3. Gangaikonda Cholapuram

D.Rajendra Chola

4.  Allahabad pillar

Select the correct answer from the codes given below:

A B C D
option_1: 1 4 2 3
option_2: 2 3 4 1
option_3: 3 2 1 4
option_4: 2 4 1 3
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • A. Chandragupta Maurya is associated with Jainism. Chandragupta Maurya is believed to have embraced Jainism in his later life and fasted to death as per Jain tradition.
  • B. Samudragupta is associated with Allahabad Pillar. The Allahabad Pillar inscription (also known as the Prayag Prashasti) details the achievements of Samudragupta.
  • C. Harsha Vardhan is associated with The great council of Prayag. Harsha Vardhan is known for organizing the grand religious assemblies at Prayag (modern-day Allahabad).
  • D. Rajendra Chola is associated with Gangaikonda Cholapuram. Rajendra Chola I established the new capital city of Gangaikonda Cholapuram and built the Brihadeeswarar Temple there. 
  • Therefore, the correct match is A-2, B-4, C-1, D-3.

question: Some Buddhist rock -cut caves are called chaityas while the others viharas. What is the difference between two ? (UPSC Prelims 1992)
option_1: Chaityas is a place of worship while vihara is the dwelling place of the monks
option_2: Chaityas is the stupa at the far end of the cave while vihara is the hall axial to it
option_3: There is no material difference beween the two
option_4: Vihara is a place of worship while chaityas is the dwelling place of the monks
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • In Buddhist rock cut cave temples, a chaitya is a place of worship, and a vihara is a residence for monks. Chaityas are stupa monument halls, and viharas are monasteries with residence halls for monks.
  • Chaityas are usually located at the center of the apse, and viharas are located on three sides of the monastery.
  • Chaityas are places of worship, and viharas are prayer halls. The word “chaitya” comes from the word “chita”, which is a mound of ashes formed by the cremation of a dead body. Eventually, it came to mean the earth mound heaped over the ashes or relics of a saint.
  • Viharas are dwellings of one or two stories, fronted by a pillared veranda. They contain large windows above a decorated main door and long rows of pillars.

question: In ancient India the territorial name Tosali refferd to..........  (UPSC Prelims 1992)
option_1: The region between the rivers Jhelum and Chenab
option_2: The basin of the river son
option_3: The delta of the river cauvery
option_4: The area of the river Mahanadi
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • In ancient India, the territorial name Tosali referred to the area of the river Mahanadi.
  • Tosali was an ancient city and important political unit in the present-day Odisha state. It was the capital of the eastern province of the Kalinga Kingdom.
  • Tosali is also known as Udayagiri, an archaeological site in Odisha. The site contains remnants of a Buddhist complex and is associated with the Maurya and Gupta periods.

question: Which One of the following does not constitute part of the veda.........  (UPSC Prelims 1992)
option_1: Nirukta
option_2: Upanishad
option_3: Brahmana
option_4: Samitha
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The Vedas are a collection of ancient Indian sacred texts that form the foundation of Hinduism. Vedic literature includes the Samhitas (the main texts of the four Vedas), Brahmanas, Aranyakas, and Upanishads. 
  • Samhitas: These are the core texts of the four Vedas (Rigveda, Samaveda, Yajurveda, and Atharvaveda).
  • Brahmanas: These are prose texts that explain the rituals and ceremonies associated with the Vedic hymns.
  • Aranyakas: These are "forest texts" that serve as a bridge between the ritualistic Brahmanas and the philosophical Upanishads.
  • Upanishads: These are philosophical texts that explore spiritual knowledge and concepts like Brahman and Atman.
  • Nirukta is one of the six Vedangas, which are auxiliary disciplines for understanding and interpreting the Vedas. While important for Vedic study, it is not considered a direct part of the Vedic literature itself.

question: In which one of the following the Gandhara sculptures of the buddha are typically Indian not greek or roman?  (UPSC Prelims 1992)
option_1: In the treatment of the robe
option_2: In the rendering of the physiognomy
option_3: In the iconography
option_4: In the style
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • Gandhara sculptures of the Buddha are typically Indian in iconography.
  • Iconography is a branch of art history that studies the content of images. It can also refer to a particular depiction of a subject. Gandhara sculptures use Greek and Roman techniques, but modified to suit Indian requirements.
  • The Gandhara school of art is known for its realism and grace, and had a significant influence on the Mathura School of Art. The Gandhara school of art is a major school of art in the history of ancient India. It is associated with the Greco-Roman style of art, but is also an intricate part of Indian history.
  • The Gandhara school of art flourished during the Kushana period. The Indo Greeks occupied Gandhara around 180 BCE, and are responsible for the Hellenistic influence that is visible in many of the works.

question: Which one of the following was worshiped as a heavenly God by ancient Indians?  (UPSC Prelims 1992)
option_1: Surya
option_2: Angaraka (Mangala)
option_3: Sukra
option_4: Sani
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

Some deities worshipped by ancient Indians:

1. Surya :

  • The Hindu sun god and creator of the universe. Surya is also considered the source of all life and the supreme soul.

2. Indra :

  • The Hindu king of the gods, god of thunder and rain, and great warrior. Indra is also the Indo-European cousin of Zeus, Jupiter, Odin, and Wotan.

3. Agni :

  • A major god in the Vedas, along with Indra and Soma. Agni is considered the mouth of the gods and goddesses, and the medium that conveys offerings to them in a homa (votive ritual).

4. Adityas:

  • Also known as the Asura gods, the Adityas include Mitra–Varuna and Ushas (the dawn).

5. Soma :

  • The sacred potion or the plant it was made from.

Other deities worshipped by ancient Indians include:

1. Agni: The sacrificial fire

2. Vritra: Indra’s enemy

question: Which one of the following is a Vedanga..........  (UPSC Prelims 1992)
option_1: Shruti
option_2: Smriti
option_3: Nirukta
option_4: Samhita
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

Nirukta is a Vedanga.

  • Vedanga is a Sanskrit word that translates to “the limbs of the Vedas”. It refers to six disciplines associated with studying the Vedas, the ancient Indian spiritual texts that are the foundation of Hinduism and yoga.

The six Vedangas are:

1. Siksha: Phonetics

2. Chandas: Meter

3. Vyakarana: Grammar and linguistic analysis

4. Nirukta: Etymology and the correct interpretation of Sanskrit words in the Vedas

5. Kalpa: Ritual

6. Jyotisha: Astronomy

  • Nirukta is the systematic creation of a glossary and discusses how to understand uncommon and archaic words. It is also called the ears of the Veda Purusha.

Shruti and Smriti are Sanskrit words that refer to two categories of Vedic literature:

Shruti :

  • Means “what is heard” or “what is said”. Shruti is a class of Sanskrit texts that are considered revelation. Shruti works are considered to have been heard and transmitted by earthly sages. Shruti is considered to be the primary authority, is timeless and eternal, and never changes.

Smriti :

  • Means “recollection” or “remembered”. Smriti is a class of texts that are based on memory and traditions. Smriti is considered an afterthought, was created by humans, and can become obsolete. Smriti gives orders and punishments if they are not followed.

Samhita :

  • It is a Sanskrit word that means “collection”.
  • It refers to four canonical books of Hindu scriptures : Rig Veda, Yajur Veda, Sama Veda, Atharva Veda.
  • These four books are the oldest layer of text in the Vedas, and contain hymns, prayers, mantras, litanies, and benedictions. The words samhita come from the Sanskrit words sam, which means “together”, and hita, which is the past participle of dadhāti, meaning “he puts, places”.
  • The Samhitas are also the oral tradition of the Vedas, and consist of several pathas, or ways of chanting the Vedic mantras. These hymns were sung at times of worship and Yajna, giving rise to the rites of the early Vedic period.

question: Fresco paintings in the Ajanta Caves were done, while which of the following dynasties was flourishing?  (UPSC Prelims 1992)
option_1: Guptas
option_2: Sungas
option_3: Kanvas
option_4: Mauryas
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • The fresco paintings in the Ajanta Caves were done during the Gupta dynasty. The Ajanta Caves are located in present-day Maharashtra, India. The caves were built by Buddhist monk Dataka during the Gupta period, when Buddhist art became prominent. The paintings are considered to be one of the greatest masterpieces of Indian art.
  • The Gupta Empire lasted from 320 CE to 550 CE. The Gupta period was a flourishing period of ancient Indian history, known for its advancements in art, literature, and architecture.

The Sungas :

  • They were an ancient Indian dynasty that ruled from 185–73 BCE. The dynasty was established by Pushyamitra, a satrap of the Maurya Empire, who assassinated the last Mauryan ruler in 185 BCE. The Sungas are known for their many wars with foreign and indigenous powers, including the Kalinga, the Satavahana dynasty, and the Indo-Greek Kingdom.
  • The Sungas are also known for having reverted to Brahmanical orthodoxy after Ashoka’s tryst with Dhamma and Buddhism. Buddhist sources claim that Pushyamitra Sunga persecuted the Buddhists.
  • The Sunga rulers helped to establish the tradition of royal sponsorship of learning and art. This tradition would be continued by later dynasties, ensuring that Indian culture remained vital and creative.

The Kanva dynasty :

  • It is also known as the Kanvavamsha, was the eighth ruling dynasty of Magadha. It ruled from 73 BCE to 28 BCE, and was established by Vasudeva Kanva after he overthrew the Shunga dynasty.
  • The Kanva dynasty was a Brahmanic dynasty, and the dynasty’s capital wasVidisa. The dynasty’s empire extended into parts of eastern and central India.
  •  The Kanva dynasty’s major rulers were: Vasudeva Kanva, Bhumimitra, Narayana, Susarman.
  • The Satvahana dynasty defeated the Kanva dynasty and established their empire.
  • Vasudeva Kanva was a Vaishnavite (worshipper of Lord Vishnu) and a famous patron of arts. During his reign, the Indo Greeks invaded, but he managed to keep his throne. He was succeeded by his son Bhumimitra.

question: Burma was known to ancient Indians as..........  (UPSC Prelims 1992)
option_1: Suvarnabhumi
option_2: Suvarnadvipa
option_3: Yavadvipa
option_4: Malyaamandalam
solution_text:
Explanation : (A)

  • Ancient Indians referred to Burma as Suvarnabhumi, which translates to “golden land”. The Jatakas, the Arthasastra of Kautilya, and the Mahaniddesa all refer to Burma as Suvarnabhumi. Classical writers also referred to Burma as Chryse and Golden Khersonese.
  • Suvarnabhumi was likely first used by ancient Indian merchants to refer to the coastal area of Southeast Asia from Lower Burma to Sumatra.
  • It appears to have been a generic name used to describe an indefinite region to the east of India.
  • Burma was also known as Pegu in ancient India and was part of the ancient kingdom of Magadha.

question: The buddhist sect Mahayana formally came into existence during the reign of..........  (UPSC Prelims 1993)
option_1: Ajatashatru
option_2: Ashoka
option_3: Dharmapala
option_4: Kanishka
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • The Mahayana sect of Buddhism formally came into existence during the reign of Kanishka, the emperor of the Kushan dynasty in the second century.
  • Mahayana Buddhism is the world’s largest Buddhist sect, and its beliefs and practices are what most non-adherents recognize as “Buddhism” in the modern era. It developed as a school of thought sometime after 383 BCE, possibly from the earlier school known as Mahasanghika.
  • Kanishka was a great patron of Mahayana Buddhism and did much to support its growth. He is credited with building many temples and stupas, including the famed Mahabodhi Temple at Bodh Gaya. He also sent missionaries to spread the faith across Asia and beyond.

question: The term nishka which meant an ornament in the Vedic period was used in later times to denote a/an.......... (UPSC Prelims 1993)
option_1: Weapons
option_2: Agricultural implement
option_3: Script
option_4: Coin
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • The term “nishka” was used in the Vedic period to refer to an ornament, and in later times to refer to a coin. According to the Rigveda, “nishka” is a throat ornament. In the Mauryan period, “nishka” was a gold coin, while “pan” was the official currency, made of silver.
  • The word “nishka” means “pure and honesty”. In the Vedic period, both men and women wore “nishka” ornaments, including necklaces, earrings, bangles, and anklets.

question: Chanakya was also known as.......... (UPSC Prelims 1993)
option_1: Bhattasvamin
option_2: Rajasekhara
option_3: Vishnugupt
option_4: Vishakhadatta
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • Chanakya is also known as Kautilya and Vishnugupta. He was an Indian teacher, philosopher, economist, jurist, and royal advisor who lived from 370–283 BC. He was a professor of economics and political science at Takshashila University in present-day Pakistan.
  • Chanakya is traditionally credited with writing the ancient Indian political treatise, the Arthashastra, which dates roughly between the fourth century BCE and the third century CE. Some scholars believe Chanakya, Kautilya, and Vishnugupta are the same person, while others have questioned this identification.
  • Chanakya was a scholar at Takshashila and the teacher and guardian of Mauryan emperor Chandragupta Maurya. He helped Chandragupta overthrow the Nanda dynasty in the Magadha region at Pataliputra.

question: In jainism “perfect knowledge’’ is referred to as........... (UPSC Prelims 1993)
option_1: Jina
option_2: Ratna
option_3: Kaivalya
option_4: Nirvana
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • Like the Buddhists, the Jains have a concept of Kaivalya which is similar to the Buddhist concept of Nirvana and Hindu concept of Moksha.
  • In Jainism, the term Kaivalya refers to perfect knowledge. It is also known as Kevala Jnana, which means omniscience.
  • Kaivalya is the highest stage of spiritual attainment, representing complete detachment from worldly desires and attachments. It is a state of ultimate liberation and spiritual enlightenment.
  • According to Jain scriptures, the soul achieves this goal through twelve stages. A soul who has attained Kevala Jnana is known as a Kevalin.
  • Jina is another term in Jainism, which literally means “Conqueror”. Jains refer to the Jina as God. A Jina is someone who has conquered love and hate, pleasure and pain, attachment and aversion.

question: Ashokan inscriptions were first deciphered by........... (UPSC Prelims 1993)
option_1: Buhler
option_2: Robert sewell
option_3: James Prinsep
option_4: Codplngton
solution_text:
Explanation : (C)

  • James Prinsep was the first person to decipher Ashokan inscriptions in 1837. Prinsep was a British scholar, archaeologist, and colonial administrator. He was also the first European scholar to decipher the inscriptions.
  • The inscriptions are also known as the Edicts of Ashoka. They were written in Brahmi script on pillars and rocks.

question: A lot of details regarding the village administration under the Cholas is provided by the Inscrptions at.......... (UPSC Prelims 1993)
option_1: Thanjavur
option_2: Uraiyur
option_3: Kanchipuram
option_4: Uttarmerur
solution_text:
Explanation : (D)

  • Inscriptions from Uttaramerur in Chingleput district of Tamilnadu provides details regarding the village administration under the Cholas. It provides information about how two assemblies
  1. Ur and 2. Sabha of the village worked and elected.

question: Who among the following is NOT associated with medicine in India ? (UPSC Prelims 1993)
option_1: Dhanvantari
option_2: Bhaskaracharya
option_3: Chakara
option_4: Sursuta
solution_text:
Explanation : (B)

  • Bhaskaracharya is not associated with medicine in India. Bhaskaracharya was a 12th century mathematician and astronomer who wrote the first work to fully and systematically use the decimal number system.

Charaka :

  • He is considered the father of medicine in India. He was a major contributor to Ayurveda, a system of medicine and lifestyle developed in ancient India.

Dhanvantari :

  • He is the god of medicine, healing, and health. He is also considered the founder of traditional medicine in India.
  • Dhanvantari is an avatar of Lord Vishnu. According to the Samudra Manthan or Churning of the Ocean story, Dhanvantari emerged from the churning of the milky ocean holding the nectar of immortality, Amrita.
  • He is often depicted as a handsome man with two or four arms, carrying symbols such as a medical staff, a book, a discus, a medicinal herb, a conch, or a chakra.

Dhanvantari is also said to have been responsible for the division of Ayurveda into eight categories, which include:

  • Shalya, Shalakya, Kaya chikitsa, Bhutavidya, Kaumarabhritya, Agadatantra, Rasayanatantra, Vajikaranatantra.
  • Dhanvantari is also said to have been responsible for the division of Ayurvedic surgery into two main branches:

1) Salyatantra

2) Salakyatantra.

Sushruta :

  • He is an ancient Indian physician and surgeon who lived between 1000 and 800 BC. He is known as the “Father of Plastic Surgery” and is responsible for advancing medicine in ancient India.
